initiatives.description:
Verso Energy plans to produce 81 kt per year of e‑SAF using biogenic CO2 captured from the Alizay biomass boiler operated by Biomasse Energie d’Alizay.  The process is based on methanol‑to‑jet conversion.
The project is part of the DEZiR initiative in the Seine‑Eure and Rouen industrial zone.  Verso Energy has launched the front‑end engineering design phase for the facility in Petit‑Couronne, Normandy.  A final investment decision is expected after 2027, with construction planned for 2028 and commercial operation from 2030.

Carbon Recycling International (CRI) has completed a feasibility study for the methanol‑to‑jet pathway, which remains under consideration for IdunnH2’s planned 300 MW facility, set to become Iceland’s largest e‑fuel project. The plant would combine recycled CO2 with green hydrogen to produce up to 70,000 tonnes of sustainable aviation fuel annually from 2029 onwards, equivalent to around 15% of Iceland’s yearly jet fuel imports. With the viability of the methanol‑to‑jet route now confirmed, IdunnH2 will move towards formalising the technology partnerships required to initiate the front‑end engineering design phase of the project.

Honeywell has announced that Inner Mongolia Jiutai Group has selected its UOP eFining™ technology to support the production of 100,000 tonnes of sustainable aviation fuel per year from e‑methanol.  Jiutai plans to use eFining to convert its existing methanol‑to‑olefins facility into a SAF production plant, leveraging the region’s abundant wind resources in northwest China.

Power2X has announced a collaboration with Honeywell to deploy Honeywell UOP’s eFining™ methanol‑to‑jet technology in the Power2X eFuels Rotterdam project, a large‑scale production and storage hub for sustainable aviation fuel in the Port of Rotterdam.  The facility will have the capacity to produce more than 250,000 tonnes per year of e‑SAF.

HIF Global has announced agreements with Johnson Matthey and Honeywell to carry out preliminary engineering for its first sustainable aviation fuel facility in the United States, planned to produce around 11,000 barrels per day of e‑SAF. Johnson Matthey’s technology will combine green hydrogen and recycled CO to produce e‑methanol, which can be used directly in the shipping and chemical sectors or upgraded into other e‑fuels, including e‑SAF. This technology is already producing e‑methanol at HIF’s Haru Oni demonstration plant in Chile.

ExxonMobil has begun producing synthetic jet fuel components using MTJ technology at pilot scale at its Machelen research facility in Belgium. To date, over 100 gallons of MTJ synthetic jet fuel components have been produced and will be submitted in early 2023 for evaluation under the ASTM D4054 process.

tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.technology_status_summary_zh: 生物二氧化碳甲烷化利用產甲烷微生物作為生物觸媒，將二氧化碳與氫氣轉化為甲烷，二氧化碳可來自工業燃燒、發電、發酵、厭氧消化或直接空氣捕捉，此製程在溫和條件（攝氏35-70度、1-10巴）下運作，無需化學觸媒，且相較化學甲烷化對原料波動與雜質的耐受性明顯更高，這可降低設備需求、能耗及相關成本。
tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 二氧化碳甲烷化在沼氣生產中尤其相關，利用低排放氫氣將沼氣中生物源二氧化碳部分轉化為甲烷，可提升甲烷產量，除沼氣升級外，甲烷化也可利用其他生物源、不可避免及捕捉來源的二氧化碳，短期內其關鍵優勢在於與現有基礎設施完全相容。
tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.technology_status_summary_en: Biological CO2 methanation converts CO2 and hydrogen into methane using methanogenic microorganisms as biological catalysts.  CO2 can be sourced from industrial combustion, power generation, fermentation, anaerobic digestion or direct air capture.  The process operates at mild conditions (35–70 °C and 1–10 bar) without chemical catalysts and is notably more tolerant to feedstock fluctuations and impurities than chemical methanation.  This can reduce equipment requirements, energy demand and associated costs.  Biological methanation also integrates effectively with anaerobic digestion by converting residual CO2 in biogas to methane and increasing its calorific value and could reduce the need for biogas upgrading.  The main limitation of the biological route is the low gas‑to‑liquid mass transfer of hydrogen, which constrains space‑time yields and necessitates larger reactor volumes.
tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.market_dynamics_summary_en: CO2 methanation is particularly relevant in biogas production, where converting the biogenic CO2 fraction into methane using low-emission hydrogen can increase methane yields.  Beyond biogas upgrading, methanation can also utilise CO2 from other biogenic, unavoidable, and captured sources.  In the near term, its key advantage lies in the full compatibility of synthetic methane with existing gas infrastructure and end‑use technologies, enabling rapid integration.  Over the longer term, CO2‑derived methane may serve as an industrial feedstock in applications where low‑emissions substitutes remain limited, even as broader electrification reduces demand for methane in other sectors.
tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.description: Biological CO2 methanation converts CO2 and hydrogen into methane using methanogenic microorganisms as biological catalysts.  CO2 can be sourced from industrial combustion, power generation, fermentation, anaerobic digestion or direct air capture.  The process operates at mild conditions (35–70 °C and 1–10 bar) without chemical catalysts and is notably more tolerant to feedstock fluctuations and impurities than chemical methanation.  This can reduce equipment requirements, energy demand and associated costs.  Biological methanation also integrates effectively with anaerobic digestion by converting residual CO2 in biogas to methane and increasing its calorific value and could reduce the need for biogas upgrading.  The main limitation of the biological route is the low gas‑to‑liquid mass transfer of hydrogen, which constrains space‑time yields and necessitates larger reactor volumes.
tech_0468_biological-co2-methanation.NZErationale: CO2 methanation is particularly relevant in biogas production, where converting the biogenic CO2 fraction into methane using low-emission hydrogen can increase methane yields.  Beyond biogas upgrading, methanation can also utilise CO2 from other biogenic, unavoidable, and captured sources.  In the near term, its key advantage lies in the full compatibility of synthetic methane with existing gas infrastructure and end‑use technologies, enabling rapid integration.  Over the longer term, CO2‑derived methane may serve as an industrial feedstock in applications where low‑emissions substitutes remain limited, even as broader electrification reduces demand for methane in other sectors.

initiatives.description: In 2022, an industrial biological methanation plant was inaugurated at Limeco’s waste water treatment plant, using technology from Hitachi Zosen Inova Schmack. Hydrogen from electrolysis (2.5 MW) and biogas are fed into a dedicated methanation bioreactor, and the biomethane is then purified and fed into the local gas grid.

In 2018, Uniper commissioned a demonstration methanation plant in Falkenhagen, where hydrogen from electrolysis (1.5 MW) and CO2 from a bioethanol plant were fed into a trickled-bed bioreactor, and in 2019, the plant started gas injection in the grid (98% purity).

In February 2025, Electrochaea and Baker Hughes finalised the basic engineering design package for a biomethanation system producing 3 750 Nm³/h methane from CO2 and hydrogen (75 MWe electrolyser), scalable to hundreds of megawatts.
In November 2024, Electrochaea and Hitachi signed a 5-year technology transfer and licensing agreement to deploy Electrochaea’s biomethanation technology, with commissioning targeted for 2027.
In March 2025, ENOSIS inaugurated its industrial demonstration project of biological methanation in France, supported by France 2030, GRDF and regional funds.

tech_0469_direct-co2-to-dimethyl-ether.technology_name_zh: 二氧化碳直接製二甲醚
tech_0469_direct-co2-to-dimethyl-ether.technology_status_summary_zh: 二甲醚（DME）用於化學工業及作為氣霧劑推進劑，且日益被視為低排放燃料而受到探索，在常溫常壓下為氣態，處理要求與液化石油氣（LPG）相似，DME可作為化學生產原料，可在不需改裝設備的情況下以最高約20%比例取代LPG摻混，並可在調整燃料系統後於壓燃式引擎中運作，它具備多項有利的燃料特性。
tech_0469_direct-co2-to-dimethyl-ether.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 在難以直接電氣化的部門，尤其是長程航空、航運與化學原料生產，鑑於永續生質燃料供應有限，合成碳氫化合物可能是少數可行的去碳化選項之一。
tech_0469_direct-co2-to-dimethyl-ether.technology_status_summary_en: Dimethyl ether (DME) is used in the chemical industry and as an aerosol propellant, and is increasingly being explored as a low emissions fuel.  At ambient conditions it is a gas with handling requirements similar to liquefied petroleum gas (LPG).  DME can serve as a feedstock for chemical production, substitute for LPG in blends of up to around 20% without equipment modifications, and operate in compression ignition engines with adjustments to fuel systems.  It offers several favourable fuel properties, including a high cetane number, clean combustion due to the absence of carbon–carbon bonds, and significantly lower particulate and NOₓ emissions than conventional diesel.  Current DME synthesis pathways include a two step route, in which syngas is first converted to methanol and then dehydrated to DME, and a direct single reactor process that produces DME from syngas in one step.  Research efforts now focus on replacing syngas with low-emissions CO2 and hydrogen.  Direct CO2 to DME conversion requires improved bifunctional catalysts capable of promoting CO2 hydrogenation to methanol and its subsequent dehydration, alongside methods for in situ water removal to enhance conversion efficiency.  DME can also be used as an intermediate for producing longer‑chain hydrocarbons, offering a parallel route to established pathways such as methanol synthesis and Fischer–Tropsch synthesis.
tech_0469_direct-co2-to-dimethyl-ether.market_dynamics_summary_en: In sectors that are difficult to electrify directly, particularly long distance aviation, shipping, and the production of chemical feedstocks, synthetic hydrocarbons may be one of the few viable options for decarbonisation, given the limited availability of sustainable biofuels.

initiatives.description: The iDME project will test the conversion of CO2 and hydrogen to DME in one reactor. iDME will use the technology SEDMES (Sorption Enhanced DME Synthesis) for DME synthesis and in situ separation of water. The SEDMES unit is being tested at TNO (Netherlands Organisation for Applied Scientific Research) in Petten and will be installed in Rotterdam, associated to a 100 kW electrolyser.

tech_0470_chemical-methanation.technology_status_summary_zh: 化學甲烷化，又稱薩巴蒂爾反應，透過觸媒加氫將一氧化碳、二氧化碳與氫氣（合成氣）轉化為甲烷與水，此反應通常使用鎳觸媒，因其活性高、選擇性佳、蘊藏豐富且成本低，運作壓力可達100巴，溫度介於攝氏150度至600度之間；超過此溫度範圍應避免，以防止觸媒失活，此製程需要碳源，可由合成氣中的一氧化碳供應。
tech_0470_chemical-methanation.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 二氧化碳甲烷化在沼氣生產中尤其相關，利用低排放氫氣將沼氣中生物源二氧化碳部分轉化為甲烷，可提升甲烷產量，除沼氣升級外，甲烷化也可利用其他生物源、不可避免及捕捉來源的二氧化碳，短期內其關鍵優勢在於與現有基礎設施完全相容。
tech_0470_chemical-methanation.technology_status_summary_en:
Chemical methanation, also known as the Sabatier reaction, converts CO, CO2 and H2 (syngas) into methane and water through catalytic hydrogenation.  The reaction typically employs nickel catalysts due to their high activity, selectivity, abundance and low cost, and operates at pressures of up to 100 bar and temperatures between 150 °C and 600 °C; temperatures above this range should be avoided to prevent catalyst deactivation.  The process requires a carbon source, supplied either as CO from syngas produced via biomass gasification or pyrolysis, or as CO2 from industrial combustion processes, power generation, fermentation, anaerobic digestion, or direct air capture. Methanation has long been used as a final purification step in syngas processing, and most modern ammonia plants, along with some older hydrogen plants, apply the reaction to remove residual carbon oxides from process gases.
Despite its long industrial history, further optimisation of state of the art methanation technologies is needed, particularly for direct CO2 methanation.  Current research focuses on improving catalyst performance, adapting to varying gas compositions, and enhancing operational flexibility to accommodate fluctuating hydrogen supply from renewable generation.  Johnson Matthey is a leading developer of high performance catalysts for chemical methanation.  This technology offers a pathway to produce low emissions methane for gas consuming applications and could provide a substitute for fossil natural gas in a low carbon energy system.
tech_0470_chemical-methanation.market_dynamics_summary_en: CO2 methanation is particularly relevant in biogas production, where converting the biogenic CO2 fraction into methane using low-emission hydrogen can increase methane yields.  Beyond biogas upgrading, methanation can also utilise CO2 from other biogenic, unavoidable, and captured sources.  In the near term, its key advantage lies in the full compatibility of synthetic methane with existing gas infrastructure and end‑use technologies, enabling rapid integration.  Over the longer term, CO2‑derived methane may serve as an industrial feedstock in applications where low‑emissions substitutes remain limited, even as broader electrification reduces demand for methane in other sectors.

initiatives.description: Since 2013, and still in operation, the largest demonstration project in chemical methanation is the e-gas plant in Audi’s site in Wertle, owned by Kiwi and with technology from Hitachi Zosen Inova (formerly ETOGAS). Hydrogen from electrolysis (6 MW) and CO2 from biogas from organic waste are converted into methane through a chemical methanation process.

The MéthyCentre project, led by Storengy, is building an anaerobic digestion plant coupled to a chemical methanation reactor and an electrolyser (0.25 MW). The plant is expected to be commissioned by 2025.
NEDO, INPEX and Hitachi Zosen had a synethic methane test facility (8 Nm3/hour) with a chemical methanation unit in 2019.
Tokyo Gas started a demonstration project on methane production (12.5 Nm3/hour) from hydrogen and CO2 from a waste-to-energy plant.

tech_0471_ammonia-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 自約100年前工業規模氨生產開始以來，氨便以液態形式儲存，最初氨儲存於加壓系統中，容量通常約為2000噸，如今常壓氨儲槽可儲存高達50000噸，低壓氨儲存已被廣泛接受，因其單位體積所需資本遠低於加壓儲存，目前有多種在攝氏零下33度運作的常壓氨儲槽類型，但目前業界建議做法有所不同。
tech_0471_ammonia-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 若氨作為航運業燃料使用，貿易港口與加注用途將需要氨儲存設施。
tech_0471_ammonia-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Ammonia has been stored as a liquid since ammonia production on an industrial scale began about 100 years ago. Ammonia was initially stored in pressurised systems, typically of around 2 000 tonnes. Today, atmospheric ammonia storage tanks are used to store up to 50 000 tonnes. Low-pressure ammonia storage has been widely accepted, as it requires much less capital per unit of volume. There are different types of atmospheric tanks for ammonia operating at -33 °C, but the current practice recommends using double-wall double integrity tanks, which can have insulation in the annular space or on the outer tank. As ammonia may be used as a fuel, new ammonia storage units may function as fuel bunker.
tech_0471_ammonia-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: Ammonia storage will be needed at trade ports and for bunkering purposes if ammonia is used as a fuel in the shipping sector.
tech_0471_ammonia-storage.description: Ammonia has been stored as a liquid since ammonia production on an industrial scale began about 100 years ago. Ammonia was initially stored in pressurised systems, typically of around 2 000 tonnes. Today, atmospheric ammonia storage tanks are used to store up to 50 000 tonnes. Low-pressure ammonia storage has been widely accepted, as it requires much less capital per unit of volume. There are different types of atmospheric tanks for ammonia operating at -33 °C, but the current practice recommends using double-wall double integrity tanks, which can have insulation in the annular space or on the outer tank. As ammonia may be used as a fuel, new ammonia storage units may function as fuel bunker.
tech_0471_ammonia-storage.NZErationale: Ammonia storage will be needed at trade ports and for bunkering purposes if ammonia is used as a fuel in the shipping sector.

initiatives.description: Japan: In 2022, Mitsubishi completed a conceptual study for the first floating storage and regasification unit for ammonia.

In the “Ammonia fuel bunkering network” project Azana and Yara are planning to build ammonia fuel bunkering units at ports, land- and- barge-based. The project seeks to provide some operational and safety recommendations.

tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.technology_status_summary_zh: 純氫經液化後，密度在1巴下可提升至70公斤/立方公尺，由於氫氣的沸點（攝氏零下253度）遠低於天然氣（零下162度），低溫儲存槽的設計旨在將蒸發氣降至最低，防止熱滲入，若氫氣蒸發，必須排放以避免儲存槽內壓力上升，小型儲槽通常為圓柱形，但大容量儲槽則採用球形以將表面積與體積比降至最低，降低熱滲入。
tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 液化氫儲存將是支持氫氣配送、氫能車隊及港口貿易終端所必需，尤其在地下地質儲存不可行的地區。
tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.technology_status_summary_en: The density of pure hydrogen is increased via its liquefaction to 70 kg/m3 at 1 bar. Due to the low boiling point of hydrogen (-253 °C) compared to natural gas (-162 °C), the design of cryogenic storage tanks seeks to minimise boil-off gas, preventing heat inleak. If hydrogen is evaporated, it must be vented to avoid an increase in the pressure in the storage tank. Small tanks are usually cylindrical but for larger volumes spherical tanks are used to minimise the surface-to-volume ratio, decreasing heat transfer. Liquid hydrogen storage tanks often feature a double-shell vacuum insulation, which minimises heat transfer via conduction and convection, and the space between the tank walls contains additional insulation materials. Cryogenic tanks are lighter than pressure vessels; however, liquefaction is an energy-intensive process compared to compression. Today, large-scale liquid hydrogen storage technology is relatively similar to that of the 1960s; however, design innovation is still needed to further scale up the tank size.
tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.market_dynamics_summary_en: Liquefied hydrogen storage will be needed to support hydrogen distribution, hydrogen vehicle fleets and trade terminals in ports, especially in those regions where underground geological storage may not be possible.
tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.description: The density of pure hydrogen is increased via its liquefaction to 70 kg/m3 at 1 bar. Due to the low boiling point of hydrogen (-253 °C) compared to natural gas (-162 °C), the design of cryogenic storage tanks seeks to minimise boil-off gas, preventing heat inleak. tech_0472_liquid-hydrogen-storage-tank.NZErationale: Liquefied hydrogen storage will be needed to support hydrogen distribution, hydrogen vehicle fleets and trade terminals in ports, especially in those regions where underground geological storage may not be possible.

initiatives.description: Until 2025, NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida housed the world’s largest cryogenic hydrogen storage tank, built in the mid-1960s with a capacity of 3 800 m³ (270 t LH2) for flight and space operations.
Air Products completed the first fill of the world's largest hydrogen sphere at NASA's Kennedy Space Center with a diameter of 25 metres, and with a holding volume of 4 730 m3 of liquefied hydrogen

CB&I, Shell, GenH2 and the University of Houston completed a novel non-vacuum liquid hydrogen storage tank concept at NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center for capacities up to 100 000 m³, which can be cheaper than conventional vacuum insulated tanks, with a small-scale demonstration tank now under construction at the Center.

tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 壓力容器是最成熟的氫氣儲存技術，涉及將壓縮氫氣以物理方式儲存於高壓容器中，供固定式或移動式（如管束拖車）應用使用，容器的壓力等級與內部容積決定其可容納的氫氣量，通常分為四類：I）金屬（通常為鋼）製容器（約含1重量%氫氣）；II）以纖維環繞包覆的厚金屬內襯容器等。
tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 車隊與平衡氫氣生產波動皆需要壓力容器中的壓縮氫氣儲存。
tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Pressure vessels are the most established hydrogen storage technology and involve the physical storage of compressed hydrogen gas in high-pressure vessels for stationary or mobile (such as tube trailers) applications. The pressure rating and internal volume of the container determines the quantity of hydrogen it can hold, and they are often classified into four types: I) vessel made of metal, usually steel (around 1 wt% hydrogen); II) vessel made of a thick metallic liner hoop wrapped with a fibre-resin composite; III) vessel made of a metallic liner fully-wrapped with a fibre-resin composite;  IV) vessel made of polymeric liner fully-wrapped with a fibre-resin composite (around 5.3 wt% hydrogen) and V) fully composite vessel (under consideration). The choice of pressure vessel will depend on the final application, being a compromise between volumetric density and cost. Pressure vessels are already used in the chemicals industry and at hydrogen refuelling stations, mostly all-steel tanks. Trucks that haul gaseous hydrogen compress it to pressures of around 180-250 bar into steel vessels (long tubes) carrying approximately 380 kg onboard and limited by the weight of the vessel. However, recently light-weight composite storage vessels have been developed that have capacities of 560-900 kg of hydrogen per trailer, increasing considerably the hauling efficiency per trip. Types III-IV have gravimetric capacities that exceed four times that of steel vessels working on the same pressure, can endure high pressures and are used in the vehicle industry.
tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: Compressed hydrogen storage in pressure vessels will be needed in vehicle fleets and to balance fluctuations from hydrogen production.
tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.description: Pressure vessels are the most established hydrogen storage technology and involve the physical storage of compressed hydrogen gas in high-pressure vessels for stationary or mobile (such as tube trailers) applications. tech_0473_pressure-vessel-storage.NZErationale: Compressed hydrogen storage in pressure vessels will be needed in vehicle fleets and to balance fluctuations from hydrogen production.

tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 吸附劑系統的儲氫容量介於壓縮氣體與金屬間化合物（金屬氫化物）之間，涉及透過物理作用（凡得瓦鍵結）將氫分子轉移至固體材料孔隙表面，並在需要時透過熱刺激或其他技術釋放氫氣，多種材料已被研究其用於儲氫的潛力。
tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 為支援氫氣分散式生產的固定式儲存，需要小型與中型儲存設施。
tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.technology_status_summary_en: The hydrogen storage capacity of sorbent-based systems is in an intermediate level between compressed gas and intermetallic compounds (metal hydrides), involving the transfer of hydrogen molecules to the surface of the pores of solid materials through physical interaction (van der Waals bonding) and the subsequent release of hydrogen, whenever required, by thermal stimulation or other techniques. Different materials have been investigated for their potential use in the storage of hydrogen through adsorption, among them; metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), carbon-based materials, zeolites and polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) have been some of the most extensively studied due to their fast kinetics, good reversibility and high stability over many cycles. However, due to the weak interactions between hydrogen molecules and the surface of these solid materials, high hydrogen storage capacities are generally achieved at cryogenic temperatures (around -196 °C) and relatively high pressures. At ambient temperature and pressure conditions, hydrogen adsorption capacities are usually very low (<1 wt%.). Some materials, such as MOFs or PIMs, can be designed, assembled and modified on the atomic or molecular levels, and research looks at designing cost-efficient storage solutions with acceptable volumetric and gravimetric densities.
tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: Small- and medium-scale storage will be needed to support stationary storage for the distributed generation of hydrogen.
tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.description: The hydrogen storage capacity of sorbent-based systems is in an intermediate level between compressed gas and intermetallic compounds (metal hydrides), involving the transfer of hydrogen molecules to the surface of the pores of solid materials through physical interaction (van der Waals bonding) and the subsequent release of hydrogen, whenever required, by thermal stimulation or other techniques. Some materials, such as MOFs or PIMs, can be designed, assembled and modified on the atomic or molecular levels, and research looks at designing cost-efficient storage solutions with acceptable volumetric and gravimetric densities.
tech_0474_adsorbents-storage.NZErationale: Small- and medium-scale storage will be needed to support stationary storage for the distributed generation of hydrogen.

initiatives.description: Europe: the research project on “Efficient hydrogen storage by adsorption with new metal-organic frameworks” (MOST-H2, 2022-2026) aims to design and validate experimentally monolithic MOFs to bring the technology to TRL 5 by the end of the project.

The Task 40 on “Energy storage and conversion based on hydrogen” (2019-2024) of the Hydrogen Technology Collaboration Programme (TCP) of the IEA has a working group on porous materials, including polymer framework compounds, MOFs, zeolite imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs), covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) and carbon-based compounds.

tech_0475_metal-hydrides-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 透過吸附/脫附進行的氫化學儲存，涉及原子氫與固體材料結構的化學鍵結，金屬氫化物釋放氫氣主要有兩種方式：加熱（熱解）或與水反應（水解），儲存材料應具備快速動力學、良好可逆性、高安全性、可負擔的價格及在適中運作溫度與壓力下的高儲存容量等特性。
tech_0475_metal-hydrides-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 為支援氫氣分散式生產的固定式儲存，需要小型與中型儲存設施。
tech_0475_metal-hydrides-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Chemical storage of hydrogen through absorption/desorption, which involves the chemical binding of atomic hydrogen within the structure of a solid material. Hydrogen release from metal hydrides can be achieved in two main ways, mostly via heating (thermolysis) or through reaction with water (hydrolysis). Storage materials should have certain characteristics such as rapid kinetics, good reversibility, high safety, affordable price and high storage capacity at moderate operating temperature and pressure. Metallic based hydrides can compress hydrogen based on their thermodynamic stability by using waste heat. It is expected as metal hydride compressor to supply high pressure hydrogen. Several metallic/metallic-based materials can absorb hydrogen with those characteristics, and hydrides of lightweight elements such as boron and aluminium and some transition metals, such as titanium and zirconium have shown potential for use as hydrogen storage materials. Metallic based hydrides suitable for hydrogen storage are elemental hydrides (e.g. MgH2), interstitial hydrides (e.g. LaNi5, TiFe) and complex metal hydrides (e.g. NaAlH4, LiBH4, NaBH4, Mg(BH4)2). Challenges associated with the use of hydrides are high weight and low hydrogen storage capacity for low-temperature hydrides and slow kinetics and high temperatures for relatively lighter hydrides. Latest research seeks to enhance hydrogen absorption/desorption kinetics at moderate temperatures and high storage capacity by adding catalysts, alloying with other elements and nano-structuring. Metal hydrides are good solutions for stationary use where the weight is not a problem and could also be used in some maritime applications. Furthermore, hydrogen storage in metal hydrides does not pose safety challenges, as it is stored at low pressure and uses rather low temperatures, and hydrogen is only released from the material when it is heated up. The use of low-temperature metal hydrides for hydrogen storage in mobile applications was established in 2003 with the completion of the first submarines of the U212A series by HDW (now Thyssen Krupp Marine Systems) and its export class U214 in 2004.
tech_0475_metal-hydrides-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: Small- and medium-scale storage will be needed for supporting stationary storage for distributed generation of hydrogen.

initiatives.description: Carbon280 launched its 100 kW Hydrilyte® Technology Pilot Plant in Kwinana, Western Australia, to demonstrate industrial-scale hydrogen storage using a suspension of fine magnesium powder in mineral oil, where hydrogen is absorbed to form solid magnesium hydride suspended in the liquid oil and released through heating.

SEDC Energy will (in the next few months) ship hydrogen from Malaysia to Singapore and Hong Kong using Hydrexia’s magnesium-based metal hydride storage containers (MHX), being the first sea transport of hydrogen in solid-state form by the company and demonstrating Hydrexia’s MHX technology.

tech_0476_aquifer-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 含水層與天然氣儲層相似，皆為多孔沉積岩結構，但含水而非天然氣，儲存的主要條件是儲層須具備穹頂形狀或構造斷層，讓氣體能封閉於結構頂部，並須有由不透水地層構成的封蓋覆蓋儲層，衰竭氣田所面臨的所有挑戰皆適用於含水層，但含水層還有額外的挑戰。
tech_0476_aquifer-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著氫氣供應擴大，可能需要地下地質設施進行儲存，以平衡電解槽使用的間歇性再生電力及需求季節性變化所造成的供應波動，同時強化能源安全，多孔儲層在提供短期靈活性方面的作用可能有限，但它們對長期儲存仍有價值。
tech_0476_aquifer-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Aquifers are similar to natural gas reservoirs in that they are porous sedimentary rock structures, but contain water instead of natural gas. The main requirements for storage are the presence of a reservoir with a dome shape or structural fault to allow the gas to be trapped at the top of the structure, and the presence of a seal over the reservoir consisting of an impermeable formation. All of the challenges identified for depleted gas fields are relevant to aquifers, but aquifers present additional challenges. Unlike depleted gas fields, which are known to be tight because they were originally filled with gas, aquifers may not be tight on all sides and extensive geological investigation is required to determine whether there are pathways for the gas to escape. With the exception of existing aquifer storage and geothermal production sites, aquifers are undeveloped, with no production wells or surface facilities. As aquifers are water-bearing, moving water can cause significant hydrogen trapping during hydrogen injection, resulting in hydrogen loss. Compared to salt caverns and depleted gas reservoirs, aquifers have the advantage of being more widely available.
tech_0476_aquifer-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security. The role of porous reservoirs in providing short-term flexibility may be limited, but they could enhance security of supply due to the larger storage capacities.

initiatives.description: The RINGS (Research on the Injection of New Gases into Storage facilities) project aims to analyse the impact of adding hydrogen and biomethane to the natural gas flow injected into Teréga’s aquifers.
Fluxys launched the BE-HyStore project in October 2023 to explore the possibilities of hydrogen storage in a saline aquifer in Loenhout, Belgium, which has a potential storage capacity of 2.4 TWh H2.

tech_0477_depleted-gas-fields-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 衰竭天然氣儲層是曾天然含有碳氫化合物、如今已耗竭的地下地質結構，可用於儲存氣體，衰竭儲層由多孔、透氣的沉積岩構成，位於不透水蓋層之下，並由不透水岩石從四周密封，依儲層大小與允許壓力（某些情況下達數百巴），最多可儲存數十億立方公尺的氣體，注入與抽取速率則受限於儲層特性。
tech_0477_depleted-gas-fields-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著氫氣供應擴大，可能需要地下地質設施進行儲存，以平衡電解槽使用的間歇性再生電力及需求季節性變化所造成的供應波動，同時強化能源安全，多孔儲層在提供短期靈活性方面的作用可能有限，但它們對長期儲存仍有價值。
tech_0477_depleted-gas-fields-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Depleted natural gas reservoirs are underground geological structures that naturally contained hydrocarbons and, once depleted, can be used to store gas. Depleted reservoirs consist of porous, permeable sedimentary rocks located underneath an impermeable cap rock and sealed on all sides by impermeable rocks. Depending on the reservoir size and allowable pressures (in some cases a few hundred bars), it may be possible to store up to several billion cubic meters of gas. The injection and withdrawal rates of porous structures are limited by the permeability of the rock, being generally more adequate for balancing seasonal fluctuations, due to their large storage capacity, and less for short-term variations. The proportion of cushion gas in pore storages is typically 50-60% of their total gas capacity, higher when compared to salt caverns. Hydrogen’s higher compressibility factor, diffusivity, and lower viscosity should be further evaluated, as it may be more difficult to contain than natural gas. Hydrogen is also more reactive than natural gas, and in the presence of sulphate-reducing bacteria reacts with sulphate-containing minerals to produce hydrogen sulphide, a contaminant, also leading to hydrogen losses. It also reacts with CO2 and carbon-containing minerals in the presence of methanogenic bacteria to produce methane. Therefore, further validation and testing, both in the laboratory and in a real subsurface environment, is required to verify and quantify seal and reservoir integrity, dynamic flow processes (important for fast-cycling injection and withdrawal performance), hydrogen recoverability, geochemical reactivity and potential hydrogen consumption and conversion by microorganisms. Advantages to depleted gas fields as hydrogen storage are that they are larger in volume than salt caverns, and their geology is already well understood from being in operation for natural gas. Compared to the development of new salt caverns, they already have a well infrastructure for natural gas, some of which can be potentially retrofitted or repurposed for hydrogen.
tech_0477_depleted-gas-fields-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security. The role of porous reservoirs in providing short-term flexibility may be limited, but they could enhance security of supply due to the larger storage capacities.

initiatives.description: Since 2016 the project Hychico is investigating the storage of hydrogen blends (10%) in a depleted gas field.

The US DOE is providing USD 6.75 million in funding for the Subsurface Hydrogen Assessment, Storage, and Technology Acceleration (SHASTA) project (2022-2024), which aims to assess the feasibility of storing pure hydrogen and hydrogen blends in porous reservoirs.

In May 2025, the Underground Sun Storage 2030 project completed the first field test of 100% hydrogen storage in a depleted porous sandstone gas reservoir in Austria, storing 500 000 m³ and extracting it at high purity in two cycles after several months

The EUH2STARS project aims to validate full-scale underground hydrogen storage in depleted gas fields by the end of the decade through multi-country demonstrations in Austria, Hungary, the Netherlands, and Spain, including four storage cycles at RAG’s Austrian site and two storage cycles at other European sites.

In March 2024, Uniper's HyStorage project in Germany presented preliminary results after injecting a 5% hydrogen blend into a depleted gas porous reservoir. Almost 90% of the hydrogen was successfully recovered and microbial activities were observed on a small scale.

tech_0478_fast-cycling-salt-cavern-stora.technology_status_summary_zh: 鹽穴是透過注水控制溶解岩鹽，在地下鹽層中形成的人造空腔，注入的水會以鹵水形式返回地表，須妥善處理，鹽穴因所需緩衝氣量低（通常約為容量的30%）、岩鹽的高密封能力，以及鹽層結構的惰性（可限制所儲存氫氣的污染），適合儲存純氫。
tech_0478_fast-cycling-salt-cavern-stora.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著氫氣供應擴大，可能需要地下地質設施進行儲存，以平衡電解槽使用的間歇性再生電力及需求季節性變化所造成的供應波動，同時強化能源安全。
tech_0478_fast-cycling-salt-cavern-stora.technology_status_summary_en: Salt caverns are artificial cavities in underground salt formations created by the controlled dissolution of rock salt through the injection of water, which returns to the surface as brine and must be disposed of in an appropriate manner. Salt caverns are suitable for the storage of pure hydrogen due to the low cushion gas requirement (typically around 30% of capacity), the high sealing capacity of rock salt and the inert nature of the salt structures, which limits contamination of the stored hydrogen. The geographical availability of salt caverns is limited. Salt cavern storage is considered to be flexible and would allow several cycles of gas injection and withdrawal per year. However, ongoing research on hydrogen storage in salt caverns is still aimed at demonstrating safe operating limits when subjected to rapid cycling with rapid pressure changes. Most knowledge of the effects of cyclic stress regimes on fracturing and fault slip comes from studies of underground natural gas storage, and there is limited data on the effects of hydrogen, which is needed.
tech_0478_fast-cycling-salt-cavern-stora.market_dynamics_summary_en: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security.
tech_0478_fast-cycling-salt-cavern-stora.description: Salt caverns are artificial cavities in underground salt formations created by the controlled dissolution of rock salt through the injection of water, which returns to the surface as brine and must be disposed of in an appropriate manner. The geographical availability of salt caverns is limited. Salt cavern storage is considered to be flexible and would allow several cycles of gas injection and withdrawal per year. However, ongoing research on hydrogen storage in salt caverns is still aimed at demonstrating safe operating limits when subjected to rapid cycling with rapid pressure changes. initiatives.description: In May 2025, the HyPSTER project announced the completion of 4 months of hydrogen storage testing in salt caverns in France, involving approximately 100 injection–withdrawal cycles under varying conditions of pressure and cycling speed, successfully validating its feasibility.

In August 2024, Uniper commissioned the HPC Krummhörn project to test pure hydrogen storage in a newly developed 3 000 m³ (0.18 GWh) salt cavern, using existing drilling infrastructure. Hydrogen will be stored at 250 bar for two years.

A European consortium launched the EUR 43 million FrHyGe project to validate large-scale underground hydrogen storage in salt caverns, converting a natural gas or brine cavern at Manosque in France and demonstrating 100-tonne hydrogen injection and withdrawal cycles ranging from 1 hour to 1 week, with replication studies planned in Harsefeld (Germany) and across Europe.

RWE took FID for the Epe-H2 project (115 GWh/3.4 kt H2), Germany’s first commercial hydrogen salt cavern storage facility, now under construction with 70% federal and 30% state funding and commercial operation targeted for 2027.
The 300 GWh ACES Delta project in the United States began injecting hydrogen into underground salt caverns to establish the cushion gas required ahead of commissioning

tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 襯砌硬岩洞穴是在變質岩或火成岩中人工開鑿的洞穴結構，適用於無法利用鹽層或衰竭氣田的地理區域，洞穴內襯以混凝土層形成平滑內壁，再襯以鋼材，由於經過細心襯砌，硬岩洞穴無污染風險，且可在比其他結構更高的壓力下運作，但須避免因暴露於氫氣而導致的鋼材脆化。
tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著氫氣供應擴大，可能需要地下地質設施進行儲存，以平衡電解槽使用的間歇性再生電力及需求季節性變化所造成的供應波動，同時強化能源安全。
tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Lined hard rock caverns are artificial structures consisting of caverns created in metamorphic or igneous rock in geographical areas where salt or depleted fields cannot be exploited. The caverns are lined with a layer of concrete to create smooth walls, which are then lined with steel. Because they are carefully lined, hard rock caverns have no risk of contamination and can be operated at higher pressures than other structures, but steel embrittlement due to hydrogen exposure must be avoided. Hard rock caverns can experience several injection and withdrawal cycles per year, making them well suited for peak load purposes. They require relatively little cushion gas, but are expensive to develop. The construction process, such as rock excavation,  is mature, but the development of hydrogen-resistant liners and leak-free connections is challenging. Compared to salt caverns or depleted fields, rock caverns are developed at shallower depths (up to several hundred metres) and require shallow basement rock, which is not always available.
tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security.
tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.description: Lined hard rock caverns are artificial structures consisting of caverns created in metamorphic or igneous rock in geographical areas where salt or depleted fields cannot be exploited. The caverns are lined with a layer of concrete to create smooth walls, which are then lined with steel. Because they are carefully lined, hard rock caverns have no risk of contamination and can be operated at higher pressures than other structures, but steel embrittlement due to hydrogen exposure must be avoided. Hard rock caverns can experience several injection and withdrawal cycles per year, making them well suited for peak load purposes. They require relatively little cushion gas, but are expensive to develop. The construction process, such as rock excavation,  is mature, but the development of hydrogen-resistant liners and leak-free connections is challenging. Compared to salt caverns or depleted fields, rock caverns are developed at shallower depths (up to several hundred metres) and require shallow basement rock, which is not always available.
tech_0479_lined-hard-rock-cavern-storage.NZErationale: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security.

initiatives.description: In 2022, the HYBRIT project in Luleå, Sweden, launched the first 100 m³ pilot plant for hydrogen storage in lined hard rock caverns, with testing continuing through 2024. The results show that the integration of storage can reduce the costs of a constant supply of hydrogen by 25-40%.

In February 2025, the HYBRIT project confirmed the successful demonstration of large-scale hydrogen storage in a 100 m³ lined hard rock cavern (around 2 t H2), which underwent accelerated mechanical testing equivalent to 50 years of operation and verified hydrogen tightness of the sealing layer. Commercial facilities are estimated to be in the range of 50 000 to 100 000 m³.

In June 2025, Ofgem’s Strategic Innovation Fund awarded GBP 500 000 to National Gas and Gravitricity for the H2FlexiStore project, which aims to store up to 100 t H2 at 200 bar (3.3 GWh) in a 6 000 m3 lined geological shat. The design phase is expected to be completed by end-2025, with demonstration planned for 2026.

tech_0480_salt-cavern-storage.technology_status_summary_zh: 鹽穴是透過注水控制溶解岩鹽，在地下鹽層中形成的人造空腔，注入的水會以鹵水形式返回地表，須妥善處理，鹽穴因所需緩衝氣量低（通常約為容量的30%）、岩鹽的高密封能力，以及鹽層結構的惰性（可限制所儲存氫氣的污染），適合儲存純氫。
tech_0480_salt-cavern-storage.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著氫氣供應擴大，可能需要地下地質設施進行儲存，以平衡電解槽使用的間歇性再生電力及需求季節性變化所造成的供應波動，同時強化能源安全。
tech_0480_salt-cavern-storage.technology_status_summary_en: Salt caverns are artificial cavities in underground salt formations created by the controlled dissolution of rock salt through the injection of water, which returns to the surface as brine and must be disposed of in an appropriate manner. Salt caverns are suitable for the storage of pure hydrogen due to the low cushion gas requirement (typically around 30% of capacity), the high sealing capacity of rock salt and the inert nature of the salt structures, which limits contamination of the stored hydrogen. The geographical availability of salt caverns is limited. Salt cavern storage is considered to be flexible and would allow several cycles of gas injection and withdrawal per year. Experience to date has shown that hydrogen can be effectively stored in salt caverns under low-frequency cyclic loading conditions. Ongoing research into hydrogen storage in salt caverns is aimed at demonstrating the feasibility of re-using caverns that have been used for natural gas and oil storage, particularly the risks of contamination and loss of stored hydrogen due to microbial activity.
tech_0480_salt-cavern-storage.market_dynamics_summary_en: As hydrogen supply expands, underground geological facilities could be needed for storage to balance supply fluctuations caused by variable renewable electricity used in electrolysers and from seasonal changes in demand, as well as to bolster energy security.

Experience to date has shown that hydrogen can be effectively stored in salt caverns under low-frequency cyclic loading conditions. Ongoing research into hydrogen storage in salt caverns is aimed at demonstrating the feasibility of re-using caverns that have been used for natural gas and oil storage, particularly the risks of contamination and loss of stored hydrogen due to microbial activity.

initiatives.description: United States: two wells have been drilled in 2022-23 and leaching has started to develop two industrial scale caverns of 300 GWh (Advanced Clean Energy Storage [ACES] project in Utah)

tech_0481_ammonia-cracking-reformer.technology_status_summary_zh: 氨裂解是將氨分解為氮氣與氫氣的過程，此反應為吸熱反應，僅在攝氏180度以上才具熱力學可行性，實務上須更高溫度才能達到高單程轉化率，提高壓力在熱力學上不利於此反應，進一步提升溫度要求，因此需要觸媒以獲得足夠的反應速率，這些限制（高溫、中等壓力及顯著熱需求）增加了系統複雜度。
tech_0481_ammonia-cracking-reformer.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 當管線不可行時，氫氣可透過轉化為氨進行長距離運輸，氨較易儲存與運輸，且已受惠於既有的全球運輸與港口基礎設施；若使用端需要純氫，氨則須裂解回氫氣與氮氣，這使氨裂解成為關鍵環節。
tech_0481_ammonia-cracking-reformer.technology_status_summary_en:
Ammonia cracking is the decomposition of ammonia into nitrogen and hydrogen. The reaction is endothermic and becomes thermodynamically feasible only above 180°C, with higher temperatures required in practice to achieve high single-pass conversion. Increasing pressure thermodynamically disfavours the reaction, further raising temperature requirements. Catalysts are therefore needed to obtain sufficient reaction rates. These constraints (high temperatures, moderate pressures and significant heat demand) limit process configurations to conventional reformer-type crackers and, more recently, catalytic membrane reactor designs.
Reformer-type ammonia crackers use externally heated, multi-tubular reactors that are conceptually similar to steam methane reformers used for hydrogen production. Ammonia decomposes over catalysts within heated tubes, typically at 600-900°C and moderate pressures (around 30 bar), producing a gas stream composed mainly of hydrogen (~75%) and nitrogen (~25%), with small amounts of residual ammonia and water. The catalysts are typically Ni-based, Fe-Co alloys or Ru-based materials, which enable high reaction rates inside the tubular reactor. The hydrogen must then be separated and purified downstream, typically using pressure swing adsorption (PSA), often preceded by the partial removal of residual ammonia and water to protect the separation unit. Energy integration schemes generally resemble those of steam methane reformers, with mature waste-heat recovery and flue-gas heat utilisation strategies, although furnace losses remain significant, at around 30% of the energy content of ammonia. While the heat supply may currently come from fossil fuels, it can also be provided by hydrogen and/or ammonia burners, and electrically heated concepts are emerging. 
This technology is best suited to large, centralised installations and has long been used in industry, notably in ammonia-cracking units for heavy water (deuterated water) production for nuclear applications, including facilities currently in operation in Argentina and India.
tech_0481_ammonia-cracking-reformer.market_dynamics_summary_en: When pipelines are not feasible, hydrogen can be transported over long distances by conversion to ammonia, which is easier to store and ship and already benefits from established global transport and port infrastructure; where pure hydrogen is required at the point of use, ammonia must then be cracked back into hydrogen and nitrogen, making ammonia cracking a key enabling technology for ammonia-based hydrogen supply chains. Reformer-type ammonia crackers are more suited to large, centralised import or industrial consumption sites, while catalytic membrane ammonia reactors are better suited to decentralised or onboard applications requiring compact systems and direct production of purified hydrogen. The contribution of these technologies to a Net Zero Emissions scenario depends on the extent of low-emissions hydrogen trade, but efficient ammonia cracking can support hydrogen deployment by enabling practical transport and storage while supplying hydrogen in a usable form where direct delivery is difficult.

initiatives.description: In November 2025, Air Liquide announced the commissioning of a 30 tpd of ammonia to hydrogen pilot plant in the Port of Antwerp, Belgium, the largest ammonia cracker of its kind, besides the two 2.4 ktpd ammonia cracking lines that have been in commercial operation in Arroyito since 1993.
Air Liquide was awarded EUR 110 million to, among other things, scale up a project with a 30 ktpa H2 (~170 ktpa ammonia) capacity under the ENHANCE initiative.

In May 2025, Uniper and thyssenkrupp Uhde announced a partnership to develop a 28 tpd of ammonia demonstration plant at Gelsenkirchen-Scholven, Germany, for late 2026, linked to future import plans in Wilhelmshaven.

KBR’s cracking technology was selected in 2023 for a 10 tpd H2 plant for Korean ISU Chemical, due in 2026. It was later awarded contracts for a 200 tpd H2 and a 214  tpd H2 plant, both by Korean firm Hanwha.

tech_0482_ammonia-cracking-catalytic-mem.technology_status_summary_zh: 氨裂解是將氨分解為氮氣與氫氣的過程，此反應為吸熱反應，僅在攝氏180度以上才具熱力學可行性，實務上須更高溫度才能達到高單程轉化率，提高壓力在熱力學上不利於此反應，進一步提升溫度要求，因此需要觸媒以獲得足夠的反應速率，這些限制（高溫、中等壓力及顯著熱需求）增加了系統複雜度。
tech_0482_ammonia-cracking-catalytic-mem.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 當管線不可行時，氫氣可透過轉化為氨進行長距離運輸，氨較易儲存與運輸，且已受惠於既有的全球運輸與港口基礎設施；若使用端需要純氫，氨則須裂解回氫氣與氮氣，這使氨裂解成為關鍵環節。
tech_0482_ammonia-cracking-catalytic-mem.technology_status_summary_en:
Ammonia cracking is the decomposition of ammonia into nitrogen and hydrogen. The reaction is endothermic and becomes thermodynamically feasible only above 180°C, with higher temperatures required in practice to achieve high single-pass conversion. Increasing pressure thermodynamically disfavours the reaction, further raising temperature requirements. Catalysts are therefore needed to obtain sufficient reaction rates. These constraints (high temperatures, moderate pressures and significant heat demand) limit process configurations to conventional reformer-type crackers and, more recently, catalytic membrane reactor designs.
Catalytic membrane ammonia reactors integrate ammonia cracking and hydrogen separation within a single unit by combining an ammonia cracking catalyst with hydrogen-selective palladium-based membranes. As hydrogen permeates continuously through the membrane, its concentration on the reaction side decreases. This shifts the equilibrium limitations and drives further ammonia conversion, thereby increasing hydrogen production. This configuration enables the direct production of high-purity hydrogen, typically at fuel-cell grade, thus eliminating the need of the subsequent hydrogen separation and purification steps required for conventional reformer-type ammonia crackers. Operating temperatures are generally lower than in reformer-type ammonia cracking, typically around 425–500°C, as continuous hydrogen removal promotes conversion. Catalysts commonly include Ru-based materials, while Pd-Ag membranes are frequently used due to their high hydrogen permeability and improved resistance to hydrogen embrittlement compared with pure palladium.
However, pressure losses across the membrane may result in the need for additional compression of the hydrogen stream. While this technology has the potential to be more efficient than conventional reformer-type ammonia crackers due to lower operating temperatures and the elimination of downstream purification units, the cost of membranes and the need for compression or vacuum systems remain important considerations. Consequently, catalytic membrane ammonia reactors are currently best suited to small-scale, modular hydrogen production, particularly for decentralised or on-board applications, such as refuelling stations and maritime uses, where compactness and the direct supply of high-purity hydrogen are advantageous.

initiatives.description: Since 2024, an ammonia cracking facility from H2Site in the United Kingdom has been producing 200 kg/day of fuel cell-grade hydrogen using a palladium-catalytic membrane reactor that both cracks ammonia and separates hydrogen in situ, eliminating the need for downstream purification and operating at lower temperature (400-450 °C) than ammonia cracking based on reforming (typically 600-800 °C).
In July 2025, H2Site secured funding from the European Innovation Council Accelerator program to build a 1 tpd ammonia cracking facility.

tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.technology_status_summary_zh: 氫氣液化涉及多階段壓縮與冷卻至攝氏零下253度的過程，使其液化並儲存於低溫儲槽中，提升其體積密度，此製程始於氫氣壓縮，並可選擇性地以液態氮預冷至攝氏零下193度，接著低溫冷卻至零下243度（包含熱交換器及正-仲態觸媒轉化），最後經等焓膨脹使氫氣於零下253度、1巴下轉為液態。
tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 氫氣液化可讓氫氣以比壓縮氫氣更高的密度運輸與儲存，儘管此技術已存在數十年，仍需擴大規模以降低成本並提升效率。
tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.technology_status_summary_en: Hydrogen liquefaction involves a multi-stage process of compression and cooling to -253 °C, so that it is liquefied and stored in cryogenic tanks, increasing its volumetric density. The process starts with hydrogen compression and an optional (liquid nitrogen) pre-cooling to -193 °C, followed by cryogenic cooling to -243 °C (including heat exchangers and ortho- to para- catalytic conversion) and a final isenthalpic expansion to bring hydrogen to liquid phase at -253 °C and 1 bar. Hydrogen liquefaction is an energy-intensive process, especially for compression. The most recent hydrogen liquefaction plants have an electricity consumption of approximately 10 kWh/kg, equivalent to around 30% of the energy content (LHV) of hydrogen, and while hydrogen liquefaction is considered an established technology, efficiency improvements to values around 6 kWh/kg are expected in larger plants. Electricity costs are only a fraction of the hydrogen liquefaction costs and the capital cost for liquefaction is also expected to decrease with further innovations.
tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.market_dynamics_summary_en: Hydrogen liquefaction makes it possible to transport and store hydrogen at higher densities than compressed hydrogen, and although the technology has been available for decades, it still needs to be scaled up to reduce costs and improve efficiency.
tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.description: Hydrogen liquefaction involves a multi-stage process of compression and cooling to -253 °C, so that it is liquefied and stored in cryogenic tanks, increasing its volumetric density. The process starts with hydrogen compression and an optional (liquid nitrogen) pre-cooling to -193 °C, followed by cryogenic cooling to -243 °C (including heat exchangers and ortho- to para- catalytic conversion) and a final isenthalpic expansion to bring hydrogen to liquid phase at -253 °C and 1 bar. The most recent hydrogen liquefaction plants have an electricity consumption of approximately 10 kWh/kg, equivalent to around 30% of the energy content (LHV) of hydrogen, and while hydrogen liquefaction is considered an established technology, efficiency improvements to values around 6 kWh/kg are expected in larger plants. Electricity costs are only a fraction of the hydrogen liquefaction costs and the capital cost for liquefaction is also expected to decrease with further innovations.
tech_0483_hydrogen-liquefaction.NZErationale: Hydrogen liquefaction makes it possible to transport and store hydrogen at higher densities than compressed hydrogen, and although the technology has been available for decades, it still needs to be scaled up to reduce costs and improve efficiency.

initiatives.description: HZDR and Magnotherm launched Europe’s first pilot plant for hydrogen liquefaction using the magnetocaloric effect under the HyLICAL project, aiming for 30–50% efficiency gains and energy intensities of 8 kWh/kg H2 compared with the 10–13 kWh/kg H2 of conventional hydrogen liquefaction. The magnetocaloric effect occurs when materials such as the LaFeSi alloy change its temperature (heat or cool) in response to a magnetic field, enabling hydrogen to be cooled to –253 °C after liquid-nitrogen precooling.

tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.technology_status_summary_zh: 液態有機氫載體（LOHC）是有機分子，可透過在特定壓力與溫和溫度下的觸媒放熱加氫反應儲存氫氣，生成富氫分子並釋放熱量，隨後此富氫分子將於吸熱觸媒反應中脫氫，此反應需要高溫與溫和壓力，以產生原始有機分子與氫氣，儘管脫氫過程中會有部分降解。
tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 使用LOHC運輸氫氣，可讓現有石油基礎設施得以重複利用，但脫氫過程需要相當可觀的能量。
tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.technology_status_summary_en: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) are organic molecules that can store hydrogen through a catalytic exothermic hydrogenation reaction at a certain pressure and mild temperature to produce a hydrogen-rich molecule, releasing heat. Subsequently, this hydrogen-rich molecule will be dehydrogenated in an endothermic catalytic reaction, which requires high temperature and mild pressure to produce the original organic molecule and hydrogen. Although there is some degradation during the dehydrogenation process, the original organic molecule is reused in the following hydrogenation stages. LOHCs must allow reasonably high hydrogen storage capacity (>5.5 wt%), and should be safe to handle (non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive), abundant and cheap. Some LOHCs are cycloalkanes, N-substituted heterocycles, 1,2-BN-heterocycles, liquid inorganic hydrides, and methanol and formic acid. Currently, however, the hydrogenation and dehydrogenation processes require energy, corresponding to around 35-40% of the energy content of the stored hydrogen, because, among other things, dehydrogenation temperatures are high. Research seeks to improve the overall efficiency of using LOHCs by looking for improved catalysts that enable dehydrogenation at lower temperatures (<150 °C) with limited use of precious metals, efficient heat management and higher hydrogen recovery rates after purification.
tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.market_dynamics_summary_en: Transporting hydrogen using LOHCs allows existing oil infrastructure to be reused, but the dehydrogenation process requires significant amounts of energy.
tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.description: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) are organic molecules that can store hydrogen through a catalytic exothermic hydrogenation reaction at a certain pressure and mild temperature to produce a hydrogen-rich molecule, releasing heat. Subsequently, this hydrogen-rich molecule will be dehydrogenated in an endothermic catalytic reaction, which requires high temperature and mild pressure to produce the original organic molecule and hydrogen. Although there is some degradation during the dehydrogenation process, the original organic molecule is reused in the following hydrogenation stages. tech_0484_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.NZErationale: Transporting hydrogen using LOHCs allows existing oil infrastructure to be reused, but the dehydrogenation process requires significant amounts of energy.

initiatives.description: In 2020, the Advanced Hydrogen Energy Chain Association for Technology Development (AHEAD) project sent a LOHC, using Chiyoda’s technology (toluene/methylcyclohexane [MCH]), from a demonstration facility in Brunei Darussalam to Japan, where it was dehydrogenated and used in a gas turbine at TOA Oil Co Keihin refinery. Toluene was shipped back from Japan to Brunei Darussalam and re-used.

In 2021, The HySTOC project (Finland) commissioned a LOHC hydrogenation and storage facility, using Hydrogenious LOHC technology (dibenzyltoluene/perhydro-dibenzyltoluene) (Germany), to supply hydrogen to a refuelling station. Dehydrogenation and PSA purification takes place at the refuelling station, producing high-purity hydrogen for fuel cells (>99.97%).

China: Hynertech carried out three trials in which hydrogen was stored in N-ethylcarbazole and transported by road at normal temperature and pressure to the dehydrogenation plant, where it was dehydrogenated.
Exolum’s Immingham trial demonstrated the bulk transport and storage of 18 tonnes of hydrogen as a LOHC using methylcyclohexane, moved through 1.3 km of an existing oil pipeline

tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.technology_status_summary_zh: 氨以全冷凍、非加壓船舶運輸，這類船舶通常設計用來運載液化石油氣（LPG），因其沸點（零下42度）低於氨（零下33度），只要貨物接觸部位不含銅、鋅或其合金，即可使用LPG運輸船，目前氨貨運量約為每年2000萬噸，屬成熟技術，研究正探討以氨作為運輸船燃料的可能性。
tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 氫氣可能以氨的形式交易，因為氨市場已存在，且相較液態氫更易運輸，貿易港口尤其需要氨運輸船以實現此貿易。
tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.technology_status_summary_en: Ammonia is shipped in fully refrigerated, non-pressurised vessels, often designed to carry liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), as it has a lower boiling point [-42 °C] compared to ammonia [-33 °C]. LPG carriers can be used provided there are no parts containing copper or zinc or their alloys in contact with the cargo. While currently ammonia shipments are around 20 million tonnes per annum (Mtpa), and it is a mature technology, research is looking at the use of ammonia as fuel by carrying ships, particularly with separate cargo tanks so that they can carry LPG and ammonia at the same time, adjusting flexibly to demand patterns.
tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.market_dynamics_summary_en: Hydrogen may be traded as ammonia, as there is already a market for ammonia and it is easier to transport than liquefied hydrogen. Ammonia tankers will be needed, particularly at import and export ports, to enable this trade.
tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.description: Ammonia is shipped in fully refrigerated, non-pressurised vessels, often designed to carry liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), as it has a lower boiling point [-42 °C] compared to ammonia [-33 °C]. LPG carriers can be used provided there are no parts containing copper or zinc or their alloys in contact with the cargo. While currently ammonia shipments are around 20 million tonnes per annum (Mtpa), and it is a mature technology, research is looking at the use of ammonia as fuel by carrying ships, particularly with separate cargo tanks so that they can carry LPG and ammonia at the same time, adjusting flexibly to demand patterns.
tech_0485_ammonia-tanker.NZErationale: Hydrogen may be traded as ammonia, as there is already a market for ammonia and it is easier to transport than liquefied hydrogen. Ammonia tankers will be needed, particularly at import and export ports, to enable this trade.

tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.technology_status_summary_zh: 液化氫運輸船是設計用於運輸液化氫（LH2）的船舶，運輸LH2與液化天然氣（LNG）相似，但由於氫氣沸點（零下253度）遠低於天然氣（零下162度），需要特殊隔熱以將高蒸發氣速率降至最低，例如使用雙層殼真空隔熱槽或膜式隔熱系統，此外LH2船舶旨在利用氫氣蒸發氣作為航程去程的燃料。
tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 在缺乏地下儲存地質條件的地區，可能需要大型液化氫儲槽以實現氫氣貿易及中大型儲存。
tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.technology_status_summary_en: A liquefied hydrogen tanker is a ship designed to transport liquefied hydrogen (LH2). Shipping LH2 is similar to liquefied natural gas (LNG), but as the boiling point of hydrogen (-253 °C) is much lower than that of natural gas (-162 °C), special thermal insulation is needed to minimise high boil-off gas rates, for example, using double-shell vacuum insulation tanks or membrane-based insulation systems. In addition, LH2 ships aim to use hydrogen boil-off gas as fuel for the loaded leg of the journey, providing a low emission shipping fuel and at the same time preventing venting it.
tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.market_dynamics_summary_en: Large liquefied hydrogen tanks may be required to enable hydrogen trading and medium- to large-scale storage in regions without geological conditions for underground storage.
tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.description: A liquefied hydrogen tanker is a ship designed to transport liquefied hydrogen (LH2). Shipping LH2 is similar to liquefied natural gas (LNG), but as the boiling point of hydrogen (-253 °C) is much lower than that of natural gas (-162 °C), special thermal insulation is needed to minimise high boil-off gas rates, for example, using double-shell vacuum insulation tanks or membrane-based insulation systems. In addition, LH2 ships aim to use hydrogen boil-off gas as fuel for the loaded leg of the journey, providing a low emission shipping fuel and at the same time preventing venting it.
tech_0486_liquefied-hydrogen-tanker.NZErationale: Large liquefied hydrogen tanks may be required to enable hydrogen trading and medium- to large-scale storage in regions without geological conditions for underground storage.

initiatives.description: The Hydrogen Energy Supply Chain project has been the first demonstration facility to test LH2 shipping from Australia to Japan, in a world first LH2 carrier, Suiso Frontier (Kawasaki Heavy Industry [KHI]), with a capacity of 1 250 m3 (75 tonnes of LH2 per trip) and double-shell vacuum insulation tanks (2021-2022).

Korea’s MOTIE launched a joint public-private task force with shipbuilders KSOE, Hanwha Ocean and Samsung Heavy Industries, and other stakeholders, investing ~USD 40 million to co-ordinate R&D, targeting a 140 tonnes demonstration LH2 tanker by 2027, double the size of the only LH2 demonstration project to date, the Suiso project.

Kawasaki Heavy Industries, Imabari Shipbuilding and Japan Marine United Corporation are starting a collaborative study on the construction of LH2 tankers, including, but not limited to, a 40 000 m3 (2 750 t H2) design. Orders are expected to be accepted after 2031, following the commercial demonstration phase running through 2030.

tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.technology_name_zh: 液態有機氫載體運輸船
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.technology_status_summary_zh: 液態有機氫載體（LOHC）可利用現有船舶與港口基礎設施運輸，LOHC可於化學品運輸船中運輸，其儲槽經特殊塗層處理，例如酚醛環氧樹脂、不鏽鋼或鋅漆，並可能配備專用管路配置以運載不同貨物，塗層類型可能決定可運輸的化學品種類，成品油輪是油輪的一種，運載精煉油，通常也設計用於運載化學品。
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 液態有機氫載體可利用現有化學品運輸船運輸。
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.technology_status_summary_en: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) can be transported using existing ships and port infrastructure. LOHC can be transported in chemical tankers, whose tanks are specially coated, for example, with phenolic epoxy, stainless steel or zinc paint, and may have dedicated piping arrangements to carry different cargoes. The type of coating may determine the chemical that can be transported. Product tankers, which are a type of oil tanker, carry refined oil and are often designed to carry chemical cargoes as well, and may be capable of transporting LOHCs. Chemical tankers typically range in size from 5 000 to 35 000 deadweight tonnage (dwt), while product tankers range in size from 35 000 to 120 000 dwt. Depending on the chemicals used as LOHCs, the type of tanker that can be used may differ and there may also be some size restrictions at ports due to safety.
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.market_dynamics_summary_en: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers can be transported in existing chemical tankers.
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.description: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) can be transported using existing ships and port infrastructure. The type of coating may determine the chemical that can be transported. Chemical tankers typically range in size from 5 000 to 35 000 deadweight tonnage (dwt), while product tankers range in size from 35 000 to 120 000 dwt. Depending on the chemicals used as LOHCs, the type of tanker that can be used may differ and there may also be some size restrictions at ports due to safety.
tech_0487_liquid-organic-hydrogen-carrie.NZErationale: Liquid organic hydrogen carriers can be transported in existing chemical tankers.

initiatives.description: In 2020, the Advanced Hydrogen Energy Chain Association for Technology Development (AHEAD) project shipped hydrogen as methylcyclohexane (MCH) from Brunei Darussalam to a TOA refinery in Japan for the first time, using 24-m3 ISO tank containers.
In 2022, the AHEAD project shipped MCH to an ENEOS oil refinery in Japan using, for the first time, an oil/chemical tanker with a size of around 12 500 dwt.

tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.technology_name: Hydrogen blending in natural gas network
tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.technology_status_summary_zh: 氫氣摻混是利用既有天然氣基礎設施，將一定量氫氣注入天然氣流中，研究顯示以體積比5-10%的水準將摻混氫氣整合至天然氣網絡具可行性，僅需相對輕微的升級，而在使用高分子管線的配送網絡中，最高20%的占比不需要對基礎設施進行重大改動，儘管氣相層析儀至少須進行調適。
tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 鑑於摻混比例低且能量密度較低，摻混帶來的二氧化碳效益有限。
tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.technology_status_summary_en: Hydrogen blending is the injection of certain amounts of hydrogen into a natural gas stream using existing natural gas infrastructure. Studies indicate that integrating blended hydrogen into the gas networks is feasible at levels of around 5-10 v% (volumetric share) with relatively minor upgrading, while in distribution networks, with polymer-based pipelines, shares of up to 20% would not require significant changes in the infrastructure, although the gas chromatographs should at least be adapted. While a 20% threshold will require some infrastructure upgrading, such as retrofitting the compressors, it seems to be the technical upper limit above which significant investments may be needed, in particular for some downstream installations and end-use equipment, although higher concentrations could be reached through R&D.
tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.market_dynamics_summary_en: Blending has limited CO2 benefits given the low levels of admixing and lower energy density.
tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.description: Hydrogen blending is the injection of certain amounts of hydrogen into a natural gas stream using existing natural gas infrastructure. tech_0488_hydrogen-blending-in-natural-g.NZErationale: Blending has limited CO2 benefits given the low levels of admixing and lower energy density.

tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.technology_status_summary_zh: 氫氣解摻混可從摻混氫氣與天然氣流中萃取供專用用途的純氫，以及相當程度不含氫氣的天然氣，解摻混可萃取供專用用途（如氫燃料電池、原料）的純氫，以及供對氣體品質敏感的用戶（如部分工業應用、原料、壓縮天然氣加氣站）使用的相對無氫天然氣，解摻混涉及將氫氣從混合氣流中分離。
tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 若氫氣摻混被用作運輸氫氣的方法，則需要解摻混，以確保需要使用純氫的終端用戶能取得管網中摻混的氫氣。
tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.technology_status_summary_en: Hydrogen deblending extracts pure hydrogen for dedicated uses as well as reasonably hydrogen-free natural gas from blended hydrogen and natural gas streams. Deblending allows for the extraction of pure hydrogen for dedicated uses (e.g. hydrogen fuel cells, feedstock) as well as reasonably hydrogen-free natural gas for gas quality-sensitive consumers (e.g. some industrial applications, feedstock, compressed natural gas refuelling stations). Deblending involves the separation of hydrogen from the methane-rich gas stream through different technologies or combinations among them, including gas permeation (e.g. polymer membrane, palladium membrane, carbon membrane, metal membranes, glass/ceramic membranes), pressure swing adsorption (PSA or membrane-PSA) or cryogenic separation, with different degrees of selectivity and efficiency. Although gas separation technologies have been used in the industry for decades, the technology has not yet been used on a large scale, such as in a distribution network.
tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.market_dynamics_summary_en: If hydrogen blending is used as a method to transport hydrogen, deblending will be needed to ensure that end users that require the use of pure hydrogen can access the hydrogen blended in the gas grid.
tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.description: Hydrogen deblending extracts pure hydrogen for dedicated uses as well as reasonably hydrogen-free natural gas from blended hydrogen and natural gas streams. tech_0489_hydrogen-deblending.NZErationale: If hydrogen blending is used as a method to transport hydrogen, deblending will be needed to ensure that end users that require the use of pure hydrogen can access the hydrogen blended in the gas grid.

initiatives.description: In January 2022, Linde commissioned the world's first full-scale demonstration plant in Dormagen (Germany), which can produce hydrogen with a very high purity from blends of 5-60% using membrane separation followed by pressure swing adsorption
In May 2023, Towngas in Hong Kong announced the successful extraction of hydrogen from their town gas stream using pressure swing adsorption technology, achieving a purity of 99.99%

In 2025, H2Site and Snam announced a pilot deblending project in Italy using a palladium-alloy membrane to extract hydrogen from natural gas blends (2–10% H2) at over 50 bar, expected to be the world’s largest of its kind once completed.

tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.technology_status_summary_zh: 氫氣的莫耳質量低於天然氣，且體積流量較高，這需要更大的壓縮功，其較小的分子尺寸也對密封構成額外挑戰，以將外部洩漏降至最低。對於相對大的體積流量與中等壓力提升（低於200巴），例如氫氣管線輸送與地下儲存，將需要離心式渦輪壓縮機，儘管此類壓縮機已用於石化應用，其效率仍有提升空間。
tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 需要氫氣渦輪壓縮機，才能讓壓縮氫氣有效移動。
tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.technology_status_summary_en: Hydrogen has a lower molar mass and a higher volumetric flow than natural gas, which requires higher compression effort, and its smaller molecular size also poses an additional challenge for sealing to minimise external leakage. For relatively large volumetric flows and moderate pressure lifts (<200 bar), such as for hydrogen pipeline transmission and underground storage, centrifugal type turbo-compressors will be needed, and although they have been used for petrochemical applications, their efficiency remains low. In a centrifugal compressor, the high-speed rotation of the impeller imposes high-velocity energy into the gas, which is then converted to pressure. Research is testing hydrogen-resistant impeller materials that can withstand higher centrifugal forces (i.e. increase tip speed) and hydrogen embrittlement.  In addition to the high tip speed and choice of impeller material, the method of mounting the impeller to the shaft would differ from that of a typical hydrocarbon centrifugal compressor.
tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.market_dynamics_summary_en: Hydrogen turbo compressors are needed to enable compressed hydrogen to move efficiently.
tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.description: Hydrogen has a lower molar mass and a higher volumetric flow than natural gas, which requires higher compression effort, and its smaller molecular size also poses an additional challenge for sealing to minimise external leakage. tech_0490_hydrogen-turbo-compressors.NZErationale: Hydrogen turbo compressors are needed to enable compressed hydrogen to move efficiently.

initiatives.description: In February 2025, Kawasaki Heavy Industries began constructing the world’s first demonstration facility in Japan for its centrifugal hydrogen compressor (KM Comp-H2), designed to boost liquefaction efficiency using high-speed rotating technology and custom impellers, with future applications planned for hydrogen pipelines

tech_0491_new-hydrogen-pipelines.technology_status_summary_zh: 內陸氫氣輸送管線的建設受ASME B31.12標準規範，儘管這是一項成熟技術，但現有氫氣管線的特性與新管線所需的特性不同，目前最大的氫氣管線直徑為18吋，而新建氫氣管線直徑可達36-48吋；現行使用較低等級鋼材（通常低於X52），而新管線可能偏好較高等級鋼材以降低氫脆風險。
tech_0491_new-hydrogen-pipelines.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 隨著低排放氫氣產量增加及運輸距離擴大，須建立氫氣管線網絡，連接資源條件良好的生產地區與儲存場址及需求中心，具大型輸送幹管的管線網絡可高效地將大量氫氣運輸數百公里。
tech_0491_new-hydrogen-pipelines.technology_status_summary_en:
The construction of inland hydrogen transmission pipelines is regulated by the ASME B31.12 standard and although it is a mature technology, the characteristics of existing hydrogen pipelines differ from the features required for new pipelines. Currently, the largest hydrogen pipelines are 18 inches in diameter, whereas new hydrogen pipelines could be up to 36-48 inches in diameter; low steel grades are used (generally below X52), whereas higher steel grades may be preferred in new pipelines to reduce the amount of steel required without compromising integrity; and existing pipelines operate under static loads, whereas future pipelines should be able to withstand pressure variations due to cyclic loading and linepack. In addition, there is no standard for the construction of offshore hydrogen pipelines and research is underway to identify criteria that will ensure the highest level of safety while reducing costs.
Hydrogen has been transported by pipeline since 1938, with the construction of the first hydrogen pipeline, made of a standard grade of steel, with a 250-300 mm diameter and a length of 240 km. Since then, there are about 2600 km of hydrogen pipelines operating in the United States, 2000 km in Europe, 400 km in China and 200 km in Korea.
tech_0491_new-hydrogen-pipelines.market_dynamics_summary_en: As low-emission hydrogen production volumes increase and transport distances expand, a network of hydrogen pipelines will need to be developed to connect areas with good resources for production to storage sites and demand centres. Pipeline networks with large transmission trunklines can efficiently transport large volumes of hydrogen over hundreds of kilometres.
