tech_0491_new-hydrogen-pipelines.NZErationale: As low-emission hydrogen production volumes increase and transport distances expand, a network of hydrogen pipelines will need to be developed to connect areas with good resources for production to storage sites and demand centres. Pipeline networks with large transmission trunklines can efficiently transport large volumes of hydrogen over hundreds of kilometres.

initiatives.description: In 2021, DNV launched the H2PIPE project to assess how their standard for submarine pipelines, DNV ST-F101, could integrate additional aspects for hydrogen.

In 2024, ArcelorMittal launched its HyMatch steel product line for hydrogen pipelines, featuring grades with fine, homogeneous microstructures designed to meet high-pressure transport requirements and reduce embrittlement risks.

In 2025, in a demonstration project in China, Academia Sinica successfully operated a 150 mm, 100 bar non-metallic flexible hydrogen pipeline, featuring a composite structure with an enhanced braided layer and functional barrier. Compared to metal pipelines, it offers lower weight, easier installation, and greater corrosion resistance.
Greek infrastructure firm Corinth Pipeworks, in collaboration with IGI Poseidon, successfully tested a 100% offshore hydrogen pipeline, confirming reliable performance at 200 bar.
Construction began on the over 1 000 km Kangbao–Caofeidian hydrogen pipeline in Hebei Province, the world’s longest hydrogen pipeline, with an 813 mm diameter and an operating pressure of 72 bar.

German gas grid operator OGE inaugurated a EUR 10 million hydrogen training centre in Werne, developed with the German Technical and Scientific Association for Gas and Water , DVGW, and technology testing body GWI. The facility replicates real hydrogen transmission conditions and train OGE employees as well as external participants.

tech_0492_repurposed-natural-gas-pipelin.technology_status_summary_zh: 改用是指將現有天然氣管線轉換為專用氫氣管線，轉換過程的主要元素包括氮氣吹掃以去除不需要的部分、更換壓縮機、徹底檢查管線及其組件的完整性，以及更換閥門與其他易洩漏部件，並重新配置或更換氣表，由於化學特性差異，氫氣可能透過某種過程加速管道劣化。
tech_0492_repurposed-natural-gas-pipelin.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 改用既有天然氣管線輸送氫氣，可避免在其技術壽命結束前除役，並減少新材料需求，降低成本並有利於環境。
tech_0492_repurposed-natural-gas-pipelin.technology_status_summary_en: Repurposing implies converting an existing natural gas pipeline into a dedicated hydrogen pipeline. The main elements of the conversion process include nitrogen purging to remove undesirable parts, replacement of compressors, a thorough inspection of the pipeline and the integrity of its components, and replacements of valves and other leak-prone parts, and reconfiguring or replacing gas meters. Due to differences in chemical properties, hydrogen can accelerate pipe degradation through a process known as hydrogen embrittlement, whereby hydrogen induces cracks in the steel. A range of solutions exists to combat this: regularly monitor the integrity of the pipeline, e.g. through in-line inspections (ILI) and pigging; apply a hydrogen barrier coating to protect the pipeline; lower the pipeline pressure until the required threshold value for safe operation is met; and minimise pressure swings. The optimal approach will depend on transport capacity requirements, status of the existing pipeline (e.g. existing fractures) and trade-offs between capital and operating expenditure. There are still challenges on the repurposing of offshore gas pipelines, as the monitoring of the pipeline with the current technology is difficult, and sometimes there is no detailed documentation on the pipeline operation over past years. There is no standard for offshore hydrogen pipelines, unlike the ASME B31.12 for onshore hydrogen pipelines.
tech_0492_repurposed-natural-gas-pipelin.market_dynamics_summary_en: Repurposing existing natural gas pipelines to carry hydrogen avoids decommissioning them before the end of their technical lifetime and reduces new material needs, lowering costs and benefiting the environment.

initiatives.description: There is  a 12-kilometre repurposed pipeline, transporting more than 4 ktpa of hydrogen in the Netherlands.

Gascade took FID on the northern section of the 400 km Flow Making Hydrogen Happen (East) project and began repurposing 1.4 metre diameter natural gas pipelines in March 2025, the world’s first repurposing at this scale, with commissioning targeted by end-2025.

tech_0493_truck-transport.technology_status_summary_zh: 對需求相對較小的用戶，氫氣可透過多元件氣體貨櫃拖車運輸，例如鋼製高壓管束，以及較輕的複合壓力容器（II型與III型），以鋼管運輸氣態氫氣的卡車，將其壓縮至約180-250巴，車載量約380公斤，受限於管束重量，然而近年來輕量複合儲存容器日益普及，容量可達560-900公斤氫氣。
tech_0493_truck-transport.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 在小規模且距離相對較短的情況下，最便宜的氫氣運輸方式是卡車運輸。
tech_0493_truck-transport.technology_status_summary_en: Hydrogen can be transported to consumers with relatively small demands in multi-element gas container trailers, such as steel high-pressure tubes and in lighter composite pressure vessels (types II and III). Trucks that haul gaseous hydrogen in steel tubes compress it to pressures of around 180-250 bar, carrying approximately 380 kg onboard and limited by the weight of the tubes. However, recently light-weight composite storage vessels are increasingly used, with capacities of 560-900 kg of hydrogen per trailer (350-500 bar), increasing considerably the hauling efficiency per trip. In addition, larger volumes of hydrogen can also be transported in cryogenic vessel trailers, which can carry around 1 500-3 000 kg of hydrogen per trip. Liquid hydrogen trailers are thermo-insulated to minimise hydrogen boil-off rate.
tech_0493_truck-transport.market_dynamics_summary_en: At small scales and relatively short distances, the cheapest way to transport hydrogen is by truck.
tech_0493_truck-transport.description: Hydrogen can be transported to consumers with relatively small demands in multi-element gas container trailers, such as steel high-pressure tubes and in lighter composite pressure vessels (types II and III). Trucks that haul gaseous hydrogen in steel tubes compress it to pressures of around 180-250 bar, carrying approximately 380 kg onboard and limited by the weight of the tubes. However, recently light-weight composite storage vessels are increasingly used, with capacities of 560-900 kg of hydrogen per trailer (350-500 bar), increasing considerably the hauling efficiency per trip. In addition, larger volumes of hydrogen can also be transported in cryogenic vessel trailers, which can carry around 1 500-3 000 kg of hydrogen per trip. Liquid hydrogen trailers are thermo-insulated to minimise hydrogen boil-off rate.
tech_0493_truck-transport.NZErationale: At small scales and relatively short distances, the cheapest way to transport hydrogen is by truck.

initiatives.description: In February 2023, Calvera Hydrogen announced that it had developed a 45-foot tube trailer for Shell, with a capacity of 1 tonne and 517 bar, using carbon fibre technology. This is the largest capacity hydrogen tube trailer in the world.

tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.technology_name: H2 blending in natural gas turbine
tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.technology_name_zh: 天然氣渦輪機氫氣摻混
tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.technology_status_summary_zh: 專門設計的燃氣渦輪機可使用純氫或富氫合成氣/天然氣混合物運行，使用富氫混合物的燃氣渦輪機已在大規模運行中累積數百萬小時運轉經驗，而使用純氫的情況下，已在預商業規模達到數千小時運轉經驗。與氫氣高燃燒溫度相關的技術挑戰（氮氧化物排放、可靠性/火焰不穩定/回火）仍待解決。
tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 支持間歇性再生能源整合的清潔靈活發電選項，但也與沼氣/生質甲烷等其他清潔燃料競爭。
tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.technology_status_summary_en:
Specifically designed gas turbines can run on pure hydrogen or a hydrogen-rich syngas/natural gas mixtures. These gas turbines using hydrogen-rich mixtures have accumulated millions of hours of operation at large scale, whereas in the case of using pure hydrogen, they have reached a few thousands of operating hours at pre-commercial scale.
There are technical challenges associated with the high combustion temperature of hydrogen (NOx emissions, reliability/flame instabilities/flashback). For these reasons, the combustor of the gas turbine needs to be modified for gases with high hydrogen content.
Burning (undiluted) fuel gas mixtures with hydrogen concentrations of 5-60% is possible in certain gas turbines, depending on the degree to which they have been modified. Burning (diluted) fuel gas mixtures containing hydrogen up to concentrations of 100% is possible in certain gas turbines with the addition of significant amounts of diluents such as N2/steam to the fuel gas.
Commercial operation of F-class gas turbines with mixtures of up to 20% of hydrogen in natural gas mixture without hardware modifications is expected by 2025. In the case of H-class gas turbines, commercial operation with mixtures containing over 50% of hydrogen is expected in new combined cycle by 2027. But many open questions and challenges remain, such as efficiency gains, NOx (which can be reduced, for example, by steam injection, which can lead to reduced system performance) and response to variability in fuel flow composition. In many cases, the H2 mixing ratio will fluctuate, and the extent to which the combustion process can respond to large variations in H2% requires further R&D.
tech_0494_h2-blending-in-natural-gas-tur.market_dynamics_summary_en: Clean flexible generation option for supporting the integration of variable renewable energy, but also competing with other clean fuels, such as biogas/biomethane.

initiatives.description: Constellation Energy demonstrated a 38.5% blending in a 753-megawatt combined-cycle natural gas plant in Alabama
Hanwha Impact completed an 80 MW hydrogen co-firing demonstration in 2023 in Korea, using a near-60% hydrogen blend.
Georgia Power and Mitsubishi completed one of the largest hydrogen co-firing trial by testing a 50% hydrogen blend in a 283 MW M501GAC turbine at Plant McDonough–Atkinson in Georgia, United States.

tech_0495_molten-carbonates-fuel-cell.technology_status_summary_zh: 燃料電池是將氫氣轉化為電力與熱能的另一選項，僅產生水而無直接排放，燃料電池可達到超過60%的高發電效率（含熱輸出的整體效率超過80%），且在部分負載時效率高於滿載，這使其特別適合負載平衡等靈活運作，熔融碳酸鹽燃料電池（MCFC）與固態氧化物燃料電池（SOFC）分別於攝氏600度與800-1000度運作。
tech_0495_molten-carbonates-fuel-cell.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 適用於備援電力及同時需要電力與熱能的應用場景，但氫氣或生質甲烷必須為低排放，或須搭配CCUS使用。
tech_0495_molten-carbonates-fuel-cell.technology_status_summary_en:
Fuel cells are a further option to convert hydrogen into electricity and heat, producing only water and no direct emissions. Fuel cells can achieve high electric efficiencies of over 60% (above 80% overall efficiency when also including the heat output) and have a higher efficiency in part load than full load, which makes them particularly attractive for flexible operations such as load balancing. Molten carbonate fuel cells (MCFCs) and solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs) operate with 600°C and 800-1 000°C, respectively, at higher temperatures, which allows them to run on different hydrocarbon fuels, without the need for an external reformer to produce hydrogen first. MCFCs are used in the MW scale for power generation (due to their low power density, resulting in a relatively large size). 
In general, these fuel cells offer a versatile and adaptable choice for producing huge amounts of power, with applications in a range of settings and industries:
- Fuel cells may be used in microgrids to provide stable, dependable, and resilient electricity to communities, particularly in rural or off-grid areas. 
- Distributed power generation, which locates small power plants close to the location of consumption, such as colleges, military sites, and wastewater treatment facilities.
- Fuel cells can also be used in combined heat and power (CHP) systems, which use the leftover heat from the generation of electricity to provide heating or cooling. CHP systems may be very efficient and cost-effective in buildings with large energy needs, such as hospitals or colleges.
tech_0495_molten-carbonates-fuel-cell.market_dynamics_summary_en: Attractive technology for back-up power and for applications where both electricity and heat are needed, but hydrogen or biomethane must be low-emission or need to be combined with CCUS.
tech_0495_molten-carbonates-fuel-cell.description:

tech_0496_pure-h2-gas-turbine.technology_status_summary_zh: 專門設計的燃氣渦輪機可使用純氫或富氫合成氣/天然氣混合物運行，使用富氫混合物的燃氣渦輪機已在大規模運行中累積數百萬小時運轉經驗，而使用純氫的情況下，已在預商業規模達到數千小時運轉經驗。與氫氣高燃燒溫度相關的技術挑戰（氮氧化物排放、可靠性/火焰不穩定/回火）仍待解決。
tech_0496_pure-h2-gas-turbine.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 支持間歇性再生能源整合的清潔靈活發電選項，但也與沼氣/生質甲烷等其他清潔燃料競爭。
tech_0496_pure-h2-gas-turbine.technology_status_summary_en: Specifically designed gas turbines can run on pure hydrogen or a hydrogen-rich syngas/natural gas mixtures. These gas turbines using hydrogen-rich mixtures have accumulated millions of hours of operation at large scale, whereas in the case of using pure hydrogen, they have reached a few thousands of operating hours at precommercial scale. There are technical challenges associated with the high combustion temperature of hydrogen (NOx emissions, reliability/flame instabilities/flashback). For these reasons, the combustor of the gas turbine needs to be modified for gases with high hydrogen contents. Burning (undiluted) fuel gas mixtures with hydrogen concentrations of 5-60% is possible in certain gas turbines, depending on the degree to which they have been modified. Burning (diluted) fuel gas mixtures containing hydrogen up to concentrations of 100% is possible in certain gas turbines with the addition of significant amounts of diluents such as N2/steam to the fuel gas. Commercial operation of F-class gas turbines with mixtures of up to 20% of hydrogen in natural gas mixture without hardware modifications is expected by 2025. In the case of H-class gas turbines, commercial operation with mixtures containing over 50% of hydrogen is expected in new combined cycle by 2027. But many open questions and challenges remain, such as efficiency gains, NOx (which can be reduced, for example, by steam injection, which can lead to reduced system performance) and response to variability in fuel flow composition. In many cases, the H2 mixing ratio will fluctuate, and the extent to which the combustion process can respond to large variations in H2% requires further R&D.
tech_0496_pure-h2-gas-turbine.market_dynamics_summary_en: Clean flexible generation option for supporting the integration of variable renewable energy, but also competing with other clean fuels, such as biogas/biomethane.

initiatives.description: In January 2025, GE Vernova completed validation testing of its Dry Low NOx hydrogen combustor for gas turbines – an advance over existing hydrogen turbines that require water as a diluent – demonstrating successful operation on up to 100% hydrogen and 4–7% higher combined cycle efficiency, with commercial availability targeted for 2026.
30 MW pure hydrogen gas turbine under construction in Inner Mongolia by utility Shenzhen Energy and technology firm Mingyang for the Otoke project.

In October 2023, a consortium of European energy component manufacturers and universities demonstrated the use of 100% renewable hydrogen in a 12 MW gas turbine in the Hyflexpower project. This followed initial tests in 2022 with blends of 30% of hydrogen that were gradually increased up to 100%, in a first for the generation of electricity integrated with renewable generation of hydrogen.

tech_0497_solid-oxide-fuel-cell.technology_status_summary_zh: 燃料電池是將氫氣轉化為電力與熱能的另一選項，僅產生水而無直接排放，燃料電池可達到超過60%的高發電效率（含熱輸出的整體效率超過80%），且在部分負載時效率高於滿載，這使其特別適合負載平衡等靈活運作，熔融碳酸鹽燃料電池（MCFC）與固態氧化物燃料電池（SOFC）分別於攝氏600度與800-1000度運作。

tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.technology_name: Hybrid hydrogen fuel cell-gas turbine system
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.technology_name_zh: 混合氫燃料電池－燃氣渦輪系統
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.technology_status_summary_zh: 在固態氧化物燃料電池（SOFC）－燃氣渦輪（GT）系統中，燃料（天然氣、合成氣）於SOFC中轉化，SOFC的廢氣仍富含燃料與未燃氫氣、碳氧化物（CO/CO2）及水蒸氣，可直接與氧氣或空氣在GT中燃燒以產生更多電力（發電輸出），若採用富氧燃燒（以氧氣取代空氣），則可在廢氣中的水冷凝後獲得相對純淨的二氧化碳流以供封存或利用。
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 支持間歇性再生能源整合的清潔靈活發電選項，此技術受惠於更高效率及二氧化碳捕捉的選項，但也是更複雜的技術。
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.technology_status_summary_en: In a solid oxide fuel (SOFC)-gas turbine (GT) system, the fuel (natural gas, syngas) is converted in an SOFC. The exhaust of the SOFC is still rich with fuel and unburned hydrogen, carbon oxides (CO/CO2) and water vapour, and can be burned directly with oxygen or air in the GT in order to produce more power (electric output). In the case of oxy-combustion (use of O2 instead of air), a relatively pure CO2 stream can be obtained for storage or use after condensation of the water in the exhaust gas of the GT. Alternatives for removing the CO2 are possible, e.g. removing the CO2 from the GT exhaust (which is the only option in the case of combustion of the remaining fuel with air), though possibly leading to lower CO2 capture rates.
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.market_dynamics_summary_en: Clean flexible generation option for supporting the integration of variable renewable energy. This technology benefits from higher efficiency and the option of CO2 capture, but is also a more complex technology.
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.description: In a solid oxide fuel (SOFC)-gas turbine (GT) system, the fuel (natural gas, syngas) is converted in an SOFC. The exhaust of the SOFC is still rich with fuel and unburned hydrogen, carbon oxides (CO/CO2) and water vapour, and can be burned directly with oxygen or air in the GT in order to produce more power (electric output). In the case of oxy-combustion (use of O2 instead of air), a relatively pure CO2 stream can be obtained for storage or use after condensation of the water in the exhaust gas of the GT. Alternatives for removing the CO2 are possible, e.g. removing the CO2 from the GT exhaust (which is the only option in the case of combustion of the remaining fuel with air), though possibly leading to lower CO2 capture rates.
tech_0498_hybrid-hydrogen-fuel-cell-gas-.NZErationale: Clean flexible generation option for supporting the integration of variable renewable energy. This technology benefits from higher efficiency and the option of CO2 capture, but is also a more complex technology.

tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.technology_status_summary_zh: 氨的直接使用已在功率容量高達50千瓦的微型燃氣渦輪機中成功驗證，在更大型燃氣渦輪機中，氨與空氣反應動力學緩慢、火焰穩定性及氮氧化物排放，是目前研究活動中正在調查的議題。
tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 轉向更大型燃氣渦輪機仍存在技術挑戰。
tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.technology_status_summary_en: The direct use of ammonia has been successfully demonstrated in micro gas turbines with a power capacity of up to 50 kW. In larger gas turbines, the slow reaction kinetics of ammonia with air, flame stability and the NOx emissions are issues being investigated in ongoing research activities.
tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.market_dynamics_summary_en: There are still technical challenges associated with moving to larger gas turbines.
tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.description: The direct use of ammonia has been successfully demonstrated in micro gas turbines with a power capacity of up to 50 kW. In larger gas turbines, the slow reaction kinetics of ammonia with air, flame stability and the NOx emissions are issues being investigated in ongoing research activities.
tech_0499_ammonia-turbine.NZErationale: There are still technical challenges associated with moving to larger gas turbines.

initiatives.description: In June 2025, IHI and GE Vernova announced a large-scale ammonia combustion test facility at IHI’s Aioi Works in Japan, with testing to begin in H2 2025; the partners aim to develop an ammonia gas turbine by 2030 under a 2024 joint development agreement.

tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.technology_name: Co-firing of ammonia in coal power plants
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.technology_status_summary_zh: 在既有燃煤電廠中混燒氨，可作為短期內降低這些電廠二氧化碳排放影響的選項，以能量計最高20%的摻混比例被視為可行，僅需對燃煤電廠進行輕微調整，20%的摻混比例已在商業規模電廠中順利達成，此外為消除燃煤鍋爐的溫室氣體排放，目前正開發純氨燃燒器，以實現高混合與燃燒率。
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 若使用低排放氨，此為降低既有燃煤電廠碳影響的選項。
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.technology_status_summary_en: Co-firing of ammonia in existing coal power plants can be an option to reduce the CO2 emissions impact of these plants in the near term. Blending shares of up to 20% in energy terms are considered feasible with only minor adjustments to a coal power plant. Blending shares of 20% have been achieved without any problem in commercial scale powerplants. Furthermore, in order to eliminate GHG emissions from coal-fired boilers, pure ammonia burners are being developed to achieve high mixing and firing rates.
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.market_dynamics_summary_en: Option to reduce the carbon impact of existing coal-fired power plants, if the ammonia is low-emission.
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.description: Co-firing of ammonia in existing coal power plants can be an option to reduce the CO2 emissions impact of these plants in the near term. Blending shares of up to 20% in energy terms are considered feasible with only minor adjustments to a coal power plant. Blending shares of 20% have been achieved without any problem in commercial scale powerplants. Furthermore, in order to eliminate GHG emissions from coal-fired boilers, pure ammonia burners are being developed to achieve high mixing and firing rates.
tech_0500_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-coal-p.NZErationale: Option to reduce the carbon impact of existing coal-fired power plants, if the ammonia is low-emission.

initiatives.description: JERA has demonstrated a 20% co-firing share of ammonia at a 1 GW coal-fired unit in April 2024 for 520 hours.

tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.technology_name: Co-firing of ammonia in gas turbines
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.technology_status_summary_zh: 高比例氨可以液態形式直接噴入天然氣渦輪機燃燒室，以降低燃燒過程產生的二氧化碳排放，儘管這可能導致比不使用氨時更高的氮氧化物排放，需要控制技術因應。
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 氮氧化物排放相關的技術挑戰仍待解決。
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.technology_status_summary_en: High shares of ammonia can be sprayed liquid directly into the combustor of natural gas turbines to reduce CO2 emissions generated in the combustion process, although this can result in higher NOx emissions than those produced without ammonia, which require control technologies.
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.market_dynamics_summary_en: There are still technical challenges related to NOx emissions.
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.description: High shares of ammonia can be sprayed liquid directly into the combustor of natural gas turbines to reduce CO2 emissions generated in the combustion process, although this can result in higher NOx emissions than those produced without ammonia, which require control technologies.
tech_0501_co-firing-of-ammonia-in-gas-tu.NZErationale: There are still technical challenges related to NOx emissions.

initiatives.description: IHI demonstrated 70% liquid ammonia co-firing share in a 2 MW gas turbine
In February 2025, Baker Hughes and Hanwha announced plans to develop ~16 MW dual-fuel turbines using ammonia and natural gas, with full testing by end-2027 and commercial availability thereafter.

tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.technology_name: Cracking of ammonia into hydrogen for gas turbines
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.technology_name_zh: 燃氣渦輪機用氨裂解製氫
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.technology_status_summary_zh: 氨可裂解（透過熱與觸媒分解）為氫氣與氮氣，使產生的氫氮混合氣體於燃氣渦輪機燃燒室中燃燒，依觸媒不同，裂解氨所需的攝氏600-1000度熱能，可部分由簡單循環模式運作時的高溫渦輪機廢氣（550-650度）供應，在複循環模式下，供應分解所需的能量則來自其他熱源。
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 開啟使用低排放氨進行靈活發電的機會，但也與其他用於燃氣渦輪機的低排放燃料（氫氣、生質甲烷）競爭。
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.technology_status_summary_en:
Ammonia can be cracked into hydrogen and nitrogen (by a thermal and catalytic decomposition), so that the produced mixture of hydrogen and nitrogen is burnt in the combustor of the gas turbine. The heat required for decomposing (or cracking) the ammonia at temperature levels of 600-1000°C, depending on the catalyst, can be partially supplied by the hot gas turbine exhaust gases (550-650°C) when it is operated in simple cycle mode. In combined cycle mode, the energy supplied for decomposition would slightly reduce the electricity generation efficiency of the overall process. To minimise the energy demand for cracking, partial cracking of ammonia is possible where the fuel mixture is composed of hydrogen, nitrogen and ammonia.
Small amounts (just a few %) of residual ammonia in the cracking product gas can lead to excessively high NOx emissions (few hundred ppm) if current state-of-the-art lean premixed combustion technology is applied.
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.market_dynamics_summary_en: Opening the opportunity to use low-emission ammonia for flexible power generation, but also competing with other low-emission fuels for gas turbines (hydrogen, biomethane).
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.description:
Ammonia can be cracked into hydrogen and nitrogen (by a thermal and catalytic decomposition), so that the produced mixture of hydrogen and nitrogen is burnt in the combustor of the gas turbine. The heat required for decomposing (or cracking) the ammonia at temperature levels of 600-1000°C, depending on the catalyst, can be partially supplied by the hot gas turbine exhaust gases (550-650°C) when it is operated in simple cycle mode. In combined cycle mode, the energy supplied for decomposition would slightly reduce the electricity generation efficiency of the overall process. To minimise the energy demand for cracking, partial cracking of ammonia is possible where the fuel mixture is composed of hydrogen, nitrogen and ammonia.
Small amounts (just a few %) of residual ammonia in the cracking product gas can lead to excessively high NOx emissions (few hundred ppm) if current state-of-the-art lean premixed combustion technology is applied.
tech_0502_cracking-of-ammonia-into-hydro.NZErationale: Opening the opportunity to use low-emission ammonia for flexible power generation, but also competing with other low-emission fuels for gas turbines (hydrogen, biomethane).

initiatives.description: A commercial pilot in Pohang, Korea, is being developed to deploy a 1 MW ammonia-based distributed power system in 2026, with the potential to scale to 40 MW in 2028 to 2029, featuring an integrated system combining Amogy’s ammonia cracker with HD Hyundai’s HX22 hydrogen engine.

Amogy and Samsung Heavy Industries (SHI) have signed a multi-year, strategic contract manufacturing partnership for the manufacturing of Amogy’s ammonia-to-power systems. Under the agreement, SHI plans to establish a dedicated facility in Korea to produce and test Amogy’s systems.

tech_0503_alkaline-electrolyser.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，在鹼性電解中，此過程使用鎳基電極與濃鹼性電解質（通常為25-35重量%的氫氧化鉀溶液）進行，鹼性電解槽是最成熟且部署最廣泛的電解技術，已在超過10百萬瓦的規模上運作逾一世紀，目前占相當比例的裝置容量。
tech_0503_alkaline-electrolyser.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 鹼性電解槽目前是最成熟且部署最廣泛的電解技術，其相對較低的資本成本有助於促成電解氫的初期大規模部署。儘管高分子電解質膜（PEM）電解槽具有更大的成本下降潛力與更靈活的運作方式，鹼性電解槽仍將持續扮演重要角色。
tech_0503_alkaline-electrolyser.technology_status_summary_en:
Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2).  In alkaline electrolysis, this process is carried out using nickel-based electrodes and a concentrated alkaline electrolyte, typically a 25-35 wt% KOH solution.
Alkaline electrolysers represent the most mature and widely deployed electrolysis technology. They have been operated at scales exceeding 10 MW for more than a century and currently account for approximately 60% of the globally installed electrolysis capacity. Historically, alkaline electrolysers were used to produce hydrogen for ammonia synthesis plants based on hydropower, before steam methane reforming of natural gas became the most cost-effective option. These systems were traditionally operated in baseload conditions to serve industrial needs. Both atmospheric and pressurised configurations are commercially available, with pressurised units typically operating at pressures of 15-30 bar. Alkaline electrolysers operate at relatively low temperatures, generally in the range 60-80 °C.
The products of water splitting at the anode may not be limited to oxygen. For example, chlorine (Cl2) is produced in the chlor-alkali process, which historically represented the largest commercial application of alkaline electrolysers. The specific products generated depend on the choice of electrolyte and catalysts used.
The key advantage of alkaline electrolysers lies in avoiding the need for precious materials as catalysts. As a result, they currently offer lower capital costs than other electrolysis technologies. However, alkaline electrolysers are less flexible in operation than polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysers, which makes their integration with intermittent renewable energy sources more challenging, and less efficient. Current development efforts are focused on scaling up manufacturing to reduce costs and enable wider adoption, as well improving efficiency, durability, and dynamic operation.
tech_0503_alkaline-electrolyser.market_dynamics_summary_en: Alkaline electrolysers are currently the most mature and widely installed electrolysis technology, as their relatively lower capital costs are facilitating the initial large-scale deployment of electrolytic hydrogen. Although polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysers offer greater cost-reduction potential and more flexible operation, alkaline systems are expected to remain the dominant technology through to 2030 due to their cost advantage.

initiatives.description: Construction started on the NEOM Green Hydrogen Project, a 2.2 GW alkaline electrolysis plant, expected to be the world’s largest when operational in 2027.

Envision Energy commissioned the world’s largest electrolysis plant, a 500 MW off-grid facility in Chifeng, for ammonia production, using both alkaline and PEM electrolysers supplied by Envision itself.

In February 2025, Nel signed a EUR 135 million grant agreement with the EU Innovation Fund to support manufacturing of its pressurised alkaline electrolyser technology in Norway, which is in the prototype phase and Nel expects to achieve a CAPEX reduction of 40-60% compared to its atmospheric alkaline electrolyser and a system energy consumption below 50 kWh/kg H2.

Next Hydrogen has developed a new cell architecture with integrated gas–liquid separation and independent fluid flow paths, enabling 10–100% operating range and power consumption of 44–48 kWh/kg H2 at stack level and 48–53 kWh/kg H2 at system level.

tech_0504_anion-exchange-membrane-electr.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，陰離子交換膜（AEM）電解槽是一種低溫電解槽，結合陰離子交換膜與鎳基電極，通常於攝氏50至60度、壓力最高30巴下運作，AEM電解結合了鹼性與PEM系統的部分優點，有望降低成本。
tech_0504_anion-exchange-membrane-electr.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 儘管AEM電解槽具有強大的成本下降潛力及高效率的前景，但目前是成熟度最低的電解槽技術，因此短期內部署可能較慢且較有限，然而其中長期部署潛力優異，因其依賴較少的關鍵礦物。
tech_0504_anion-exchange-membrane-electr.technology_status_summary_en:
Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). Anion exchange membrane (AEM) electrolysers are a type of low-temperature electrolyser that combine an anion exchange membrane with nickel-based electrodes. They typically operate at temperatures between 50 °C and 60 °C and at pressures of up to 30 bar.
AEM electrolysis combines some of the benefits of alkaline and PEM systems, potentially reducing the cost of hydrogen production. Since AEM electrolysers operate in an alkaline environment, they can use non-platinum group metal catalysts, such as nickel alloys and nickel-, iron-, or cobalt-based oxides. This also enables the use of low-cost stainless steel current collectors and nickel-based porous transport layers, instead of the expensive platinum-coated titanium required in PEM electrolysers, potentially leading to lower capital costs. Compared with alkaline electrolysers, a key advantage is that the anion exchange membrane acts as a solid electrolyte, eliminating the need for highly concentrated (25-35 wt% KOH) corrosive liquid electrolytes. AEM electrolysers can operate efficiently with dilute feed solutions (below 1 M KOH), reducing corrosion, maintenance costs, and safety risks. While operation with a pure water feed is possible, the low ionic conductivity of current membranes and ionomers generally results in low performance.  Overall, AEM electrolysers are emerging as an attractive option to reduce the cost of hydrogen production, with the first small-scale applications now coming online. However, significant development is still required in terms of improving performance (efficient operation at high current densities), increase durability, and scale up the cell area.
tech_0504_anion-exchange-membrane-electr.market_dynamics_summary_en: Although AEM electrolysers offer strong cost-reduction potential and the prospect of high efficiencies, they are currently the least mature electrolyser technology and are therefore likely to see slower and more limited deployment in the near term. However, they offer excellent medium- to long-term deployment potential, since they rely on fewer critical minerals and operate in a less corrosive environment than other electrolysis designs, potentially reducing degradation to increase the operating lifetime.

initiatives.description: Between 2023 and 2024, Enapter delivered AEM electrolysers to multiple projects globally, including several above 1 MW, with one already operating in Germany and two others under construction in the UK and the Netherlands.

Enapter has expanded its portfolio with its first multi-megawatt AEM electrolyser, the Nexus 2500, featuring a capacity of 2.5 MW with the same footprint as the company’s 1 MW system. With a multicore design, it enables flexible operation and easier integration with intermittent renewables.

tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.technology_name_zh: 高分子電解質膜電解槽
tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，高分子電解質膜（PEM）電解槽使用質子導電固態高分子電解質形成電解槽，並分別於陰極與陽極分離氫氣與氧氣的產生反應。PEM電解槽與鹼性電解槽是目前兩種商業成熟的電解技術之一，相較其他技術反應更靈敏。
tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 儘管高分子電解質膜（PEM）電解槽目前的部署程度不及鹼性電解槽，但已有多項電解專案採用此技術。由於其在動態條件下的優異性能，此技術可能在促成電解氫生產整合方面扮演重要角色。
tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.technology_status_summary_en:
Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). Polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysers use a proton-conducting solid polymer electrolyte to form the electrolysis cell and separate the H2 and O2 evolution reactions at the cathode and anode, respectively.
PEM electrolysers represent one of the two commercially mature electrolysis technologies, together with alkaline electrolysers. Compared with the latter, PEM electrolysers require more expensive materials, including noble-metal catalysts (platinum and iridium), titanium bipolar plates, and specialised polymer membrane materials, resulting in higher capital costs. Nevertheless, PEM electrolysers offer a greater cost-reduction potential and several operational advantages. They can operate efficiently under dynamic conditions and at partial loads, with rapid response times and low degradation rates associated with load variations. These characteristics make them particularly well suited for integration with intermittent renewable energy sources and unlock the possibility to provide grid services, such as frequency regulation. In addition, PEM electrolysers can operate at higher pressures (typically 30–80 bar), reducing downstream compression requirements, and can achieve higher current and power densities, leading to more compact system designs. The lifetime of PEM electrolysers is currently slightly lower than that of alkaline systems, but technological developments are expected to close this gap. Extensive research is currently underway to improve durability and develop acid-stable electrolysers to mitigate reliance on noble metals, thus reducing costs.
tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.market_dynamics_summary_en: Although polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysers are currently less widely deployed than alkaline electrolysers, several electrolysis projects are based on this technology. As a result of their superior performance under dynamic conditions, this technology may play an important role in enabling the integration of electrolytic hydrogen production with intermittent renewable energy sources.
tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.description:

tech_0505_polymer-electrolyte-membrane-e.NZErationale: Although polymer electrolyte membrane (PEM) electrolysers are currently less widely deployed than alkaline electrolysers, several electrolysis projects are based on this technology. As a result of their superior performance under dynamic conditions, this technology may play an important role in enabling the integration of electrolytic hydrogen production with intermittent renewable energy sources.

initiatives.description: Envision Energy commissioned the world’s largest electrolysis plant, a 500 MW off-grid facility in Chifeng, for ammonia production, using both alkaline and PEM electrolysers supplied by Envision itself.

ITM Power reduces iridium use by 40% further in its stack while maintaining performance and lifetime. ITM Power had already reduced precious metal use by over 80% in the past years, already meeting the EU 2030 precious metal loading target for PEM electrolysers in 2019.

Calicat announced that its non-iridium PEM electrolyser catalyst sustained current densities above 2 A/cm² at a cell voltage of 2 V, a performance level typically requiring iridium. The catalyst demonstrated stable operation over 15 000 hours in a durability experiment, overcoming the durability issue observed in most non-iridium catalysts under real-world conditions.

Sweden-based Smoltek Hydrogen has announced a new PEM electrolyser design with a novel porous transfer electrode (PTE) that reduces iridium use by up to 95% compared to conventional technology. The technology has been tested for more than 250 operating hours and could significantly lower costs.

Researchers at Tohoku University have developed a durable non-noble metal-based cathode via a surface reconstruction pathway. The cathode has demonstrated stable performance for more than 300 hours and, if commercialised, could significantly reduce the cost of hydrogen production.

tech_0506_solid-oxide-electrolyser-cell.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，固態氧化物電解槽（SOEC）在攝氏600至900度的高運作溫度下進行此過程，SOEC使用蒸汽而非液態水運作，並採用陶瓷固態氧化物電解質，蒸汽供應至陰極（燃料電極），在此電化學還原為氫氣（H2）與氧離子。
tech_0506_solid-oxide-electrolyser-cell.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 固態氧化物電解槽目前成熟度低於鹼性與PEM電解槽，成本也較高，但具有強大的成本下降潛力，且在有適當熱源可供應部分電解所需能量時，可達到更高的效率。其可逆運作為燃料電池的能力也具有優勢。
tech_0506_solid-oxide-electrolyser-cell.technology_status_summary_en:
Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). In a solid oxide electrolyser cell (SOEC), this process takes place at high operating temperatures, typically between 600 °C and 900 °C. SOECs operate with steam instead of liquid water and use a ceramic solid oxide electrolyte. Steam is supplied at the cathode (fuel electrode), where it is electrochemically reduced to hydrogen (H2) and oxygen ions (O2-). These ions migrate through the ceramic electrolyte towards the anode (air electrode), where they combine to form molecular oxygen (O2). 
Compared with low-temperature electrolysis, the high operating temperature enables favourable thermodynamics, faster reaction kinetics, and lower ohmic losses. This makes SOECs the most efficient hydrogen production technology, although it limits their operational flexibility. While SOECs can load follow very well and have rapid response, they have slow ramp rates from cold start due to the need to reach high temperatures and avoid thermal shocks for the ceramic electrolyte. As a result, they need to operate in a “hot idle” mode.
As SOECs require a source of heat to produce steam, they are especially attractive for the co-location and integration with other industrial or chemical processes, where excess heat can be recovered to further improve the system efficiency. Possible heat sources include waste heat from synthetic hydrocarbon synthesis, industrial waste heat, solar thermal, nuclear power, or geothermal energy. SOECs can also operate in reverse mode as fuel cells in so-called reversible solid oxide cells. If combined with hydrogen storage, this unlocks the possibility to provide balancing service or seasonal storage of renewable electricity. In addition, SOECs can be used for the co-electrolysis of steam and CO2, producing a syngas mixture that can be subsequently converted into a synthetic fuel. Lower-temperature SOECs operating at temperatures between 500 °C and 600 °C are also being explored, offering the advantage of using metal-supported cells, with potentially lower costs and higher robustness.
Although SOECs rely on ceramic materials and use fewer rare materials compared to PEM electrolysers, their capital costs are currently higher than those of low-temperature electrolysers. SOEC systems have been tested in large demonstration plants, and different multi-MW scale projects have now started operation. Ongoing research is focused on improving system durability while maintaining high performance.
tech_0506_solid-oxide-electrolyser-cell.market_dynamics_summary_en: Solid oxide electrolysers are currently less mature and more expensive than alkaline and PEM electrolysers, but they offer strong cost-reduction potential and can achieve higher efficiencies, particularly where suitable heat sources are available to supply part of the energy required for electrolysis. Their ability to operate reversibly as fuel cells also provides flexibility for future integrated energy systems. Their relevance in a Net Zero Emissions scenario is therefore strongest in industrial or energy systems where low-emissions heat can be integrated, while their efficiency advantage is reduced in applications where such heat sources are not available and must be provided electrically.

initiatives.description: Topsoe opened its first, and Europe’s largest, SOEC manufacturing facility in Herning, Denmark, a plant with a 500 MW annual capacity that is supported by EUR 94 million from the EU Innovation Fund.

As of May 2025, Topsoe had completed more than 10 000-hour demonstration of SOEC stacks under industrial conditions at its research centre in Denmark, confirming stable performance under fluctuating operation with a stack power consumption of 36 kWh/kg H2.
Topsoe secured a firm order to supply 100 MW of SOEC electrolysers to First Ammonia in the United States, marking the first large-scale deployment of the technology.
In October 2024, the EU awarded thyssenkrupp nucera up to EUR 36 million for constructing a 300 MW/year SOEC manufacturing plant in Germany

Hadean Energy’s tubular SOEC operated for 1 000 hours in a trial in Australia, using steam from a steel plant and with a power consumption <36 kWh/kg H2. A 5 kW demonstration is planned in United Kingdom.

The EU-funded MULTIPLHY project has started operation of a 2.6 MW SOEC electrolyser at Neste’s renewable products refinery in Rotterdam. Supplied by Sunfire, the high-temperature electrolyser is integrated into the refinery’s processes and operates at temperatures as high as 850 °C, demonstrating the viability of SOEC-based renewable hydrogen production in an industrial environment.

tech_0507_seawater-electrolysis.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，海水電解正被研究用以解決淡水供應問題，有望降低製氫成本並實現更多元的應用，然而由於多項技術挑戰，以海水作為電解質的應用仍處於發展早期階段，海水成分高度複雜且不均勻。
tech_0507_seawater-electrolysis.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 直接海水電解目前處於發展非常早期的階段，效率低於其他電解技術（包括間接海水電解，後者短期內可能是較優先的選項），長期而言，直接海水電解可能在缺水地區的製氫方面扮演相關角色。
tech_0507_seawater-electrolysis.technology_status_summary_en:
Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). Seawater electrolysis is being investigated to address the issue of freshwater availability, potentially reducing hydrogen production costs and enabling more versatile applications. However, the use of seawater as electrolyte is still at the early stages of development due to several technical challenges. Seawater has a highly complex and heterogeneous composition, with pH levels ranging from acidic to alkaline depending on location and season. It usually has high concentrations of impurities, including dissolved inorganic salts, organic molecules, living microorganisms, and dissolved gases, which create significant challenges related to corrosion, occurrence of side reactions, and degradation of membrane and electrodes. In addition, the presence of ions leads to lower conductivity and, therefore, reduced conversion efficiency.
To address these issues, indirect seawater electrolysis (ISE), which includes a preliminary desalination stage, is currently favoured over direct seawater electrolysis (DSE). In this process, seawater is first desalinated, often via reverse osmosis, and then fed to a conventional electrolyser. Compared with DSE, ISE relies on two mature technologies. Although the additional desalination step increases costs and efficiency losses, its impact on the overall cost of hydrogen production is generally limited. As a result, most projects under development today focus on indirect seawater electrolysis. Nevertheless, research in direct seawater electrolysis remains very active, driven by the potential to reduce system complexity and footprint, which could facilitate integration with offshore renewable energy installations, and reflected in the first pilot projects that have begun operating in recent years.
tech_0507_seawater-electrolysis.market_dynamics_summary_en: Direct seawater electrolysis is at a very early stage of development and currently presents lower efficiencies and higher costs than other electrolysis technologies, including indirect seawater electrolysis, which is likely to be the preferred option in the near term. Over the longer term, it could play a relevant role for hydrogen generation in water-stressed areas with high renewable energy potential or in offshore applications, potentially offering lower system complexity and smaller footprint and land use than indirect seawater electrolysis.

Although the additional desalination step increases costs and efficiency losses, its impact on the overall cost of hydrogen production is generally limited. As a result, most projects under development today focus on indirect seawater electrolysis. Nevertheless, research in direct seawater electrolysis remains very active, driven by the potential to reduce system complexity and footprint, which could facilitate integration with offshore renewable energy installations, and reflected in the first pilot projects that have begun operating in recent years.
tech_0507_seawater-electrolysis.NZErationale: Direct seawater electrolysis is at a very early stage of development and currently presents lower efficiencies and higher costs than other electrolysis technologies, including indirect seawater electrolysis, which is likely to be the preferred option in the near term. Over the longer term, it could play a relevant role for hydrogen generation in water-stressed areas with high renewable energy potential or in offshore applications, potentially offering lower system complexity and smaller footprint and land use than indirect seawater electrolysis.

initiatives.description: Researchers from Standford University, University of Oregon and Manchester Metropolitan University have developed a new system for the production of hydrogen directly from seawater
Researchers from the University of Adelaide have developed a novel catalyst for PEM electrolysers able to operate with seawater with minimal efficiency penalty
In December 2024, Sinopec completed a 100 kW pilot project at its Qingdao refinery producing hydrogen directly from seawater, employing chlorine-resistant electrodes.
In December 2024, the world’s first MW-scale seawater electrolyser was put into trial operation by the China National Offshore Oil Corporation (CNOOC), and it successfully completed a test run.

Latent Drive has received a £630,000 grant to trial its SeaStack electrolyser at Portland Port, where it will produce green hydrogen directly from seawater to fuel a harbour patrol vessel. The project aims to decarbonise port operations while demonstrating the potential of offshore hydrogen production.

tech_0508_waste-water-electrolysis.technology_status_summary_zh: 水電解是一種利用電力將水（H2O）分解為基本成分——氫氣（H2）與氧氣（O2）的電化學製程，以廢水取代純化水作為原料的做法，正從循環經濟角度被研究，以解決淡水供應問題，將製氫與廢水處理相結合，廢水電解可依進入電解槽前原料的物理化學性質分類。
tech_0508_waste-water-electrolysis.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 廢水電解目前處於發展非常早期的階段，效率低於其他電解技術，成本也較高，長期而言，其可能在缺水地區的製氫方面扮演相關角色，限制淡水消耗並支持更循環的經濟。
tech_0508_waste-water-electrolysis.technology_status_summary_en: Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). The use of wastewater instead of purified water as feedstock is being investigated to address the issue of freshwater availability in a circular economy perspective, combining hydrogen production with wastewater treatment. Wastewater electrolysis can be classified according to the physicochemical properties of the feedstock before entering the electrolysis cell. One option is to pretreat wastewater before feeding it to a conventional electrolyser, for example through forward osmosis. Alternatively, untreated wastewater can be fed directly to a microbial electrolysis cell. In this process, organic matter is oxidised at the anode via electrochemically active exoelectrogenic bacteria (exoelectrogens), i.e., microorganisms capable of transferring electrons extracellularly, while hydrogen is produced at the cathode, under an externally applied electric field. Both approaches are still at an early stage of development and have so far been demonstrated mainly at laboratory scale. Current research is focused on scaling up these technologies and validating their performance under real wastewater conditions, where feedstock properties can vary significantly.
tech_0508_waste-water-electrolysis.market_dynamics_summary_en: Wastewater electrolysis is at a very early stage of development and currently presents lower efficiencies and higher costs than other electrolysis technologies. In the long term, it could play a relevant role for hydrogen generation in water-stressed areas, limiting the consumption of freshwater and supporting a more circular economy.
tech_0508_waste-water-electrolysis.description: Water electrolysis is an electrochemical process that uses electricity to split water (H2O) into its basic components: hydrogen (H2) and oxygen (O2). initiatives.description: Spain: The GREENLYSIS Project gathered different innovative technologies and combined them to construct a prototype plant in Spain, able to produce hydrogen and oxygen using wastewater coming from an urban wastewater treatment plant (WWTP). The core process was the electrolysis, and the entire plant was fed with renewable energy. The data obtained demonstrated the technical viability of the entire pilot plant to produce hydrogen and oxygen using only renewable energy

A cost-benefit analysis for  hydrogen production from treated effluent in Oman was conducted using the Al Ansab sewage treatment plant in the governorate of Muscat. The results have shown that, considering sustainable alternatives to produce hydrogen from a proton exchange membrane (PEM) water electrolyser system for two cases with capacities of 1 500 kg H2/day and 50 000 kg H2/day (water consumption in the systems was 27m3/day and 900 m3/day, respectively),.

Researchers at the Korea Institute of Materials Science have developed a wastewater electrolysis system that has been tested for more than 2000 operating hours and is capable of producing hydrogen from industrial waste alkaline water generated by semiconductor manufacturing processes.

Scientists at Princeton University have developed a method that allows reclaimed wastewater to be used in a PEM electrolyser. The approach involves acidifying the wastewater feed and has been demonstrated in the laboratory for more than 300 operating hours without issues.
METzero has received £1.6 million to support the first commercial-scale deployment of its microbial electrolysis technology as part of the METRAU project.

tech_0509_in-situ-hydrocarbon-conversion.technology_status_summary_zh: 刺激型地質氫是指透過刻意的人為介入在地下產生的氫氣，與透過自然發生的地下過程產生的天然地質氫形成對比，在此路徑中，碳氫化合物在含碳氫化合物地層內直接轉化以產生氫氣，氫氣被回收至地表，而二氧化碳則保留於現地或透過再注入封存，現地碳氫化合物轉化可能提供一種替代方案。
tech_0509_in-situ-hydrocarbon-conversion.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 地質氫正被探索作為以化石為基礎的製氫的替代方案，後者即使二氧化碳經減排，仍涉及燃料供應鏈的上游與中游排放，也是電解氫生產或其他可能涉及高生產成本路徑的替代方案，儘管多種地質氫路徑已經過測試。
tech_0509_in-situ-hydrocarbon-conversion.technology_status_summary_en:
Stimulated geologic hydrogen refers to hydrogen generated in the subsurface through deliberate anthropogenic interventions, in contrast to natural geologic hydrogen, which is produced through naturally occurring subsurface processes. In this pathway, hydrocarbons are converted directly within hydrocarbon-bearing formations to produce hydrogen, which is recovered to the surface, while CO2 is retained in situ or sequestered through reinjection. In situ hydrocarbon conversion may offer an alternative where conventional hydrocarbon production is technically challenging or uneconomic, such as in heavy or extra-heavy oil reservoirs, tight formations or depleted fields. The use of existing wells and subsurface infrastructure can lower capital intensity and shorten deployment timelines by enabling the repurposing of otherwise stranded assets. In situ hydrogen conversion pathways are typically grouped into thermochemical and biological approaches.
Thermochemical pathways include controlled partial oxidation, steam reforming and pyrolysis, often catalytically assisted. Concepts with relatively higher near-term maturity include in situ combustion of oil and tar sands, underground coal gasification, and steam-assisted gravity drainage-based of tar sands, all of which have been tested in subsurface environments. 
Biological pathways involve bio-hydrogenogenesis, using indigenous or augmented microbial communities to generate hydrogen in the subsurface by introducing hydrogen-producing microorganisms and/or nutrients into hydrocarbon-bearing formations, or by adjusting environmental conditions such as pH to enhance microbial hydrogen generation. Several approaches have been tested in subsurface environments, including biological methanotrophic conversion of natural gas, microbial degradation of residual hydrocarbons in oil fields, and bioaugmentation with hydrogen-producing microorganisms.
tech_0509_in-situ-hydrocarbon-conversion.market_dynamics_summary_en: Geologic hydrogen is being explored as an alternative to fossil-based hydrogen production, which would involve upstream and midstream emissions from fuel supply chains even when CO2 is abated, as well as to electrolytic hydrogen production or other routes that may entail high production costs. While several geologic hydrogen pathways have been tested in subsurface environments, they remain at an early stage of development, with significant uncertainties related to scalability, long-term production performance and economic viability. As a result, the role of geologic hydrogen remains uncertain and is likely to be limited in the near to medium term. Natural hydrogen extraction may involve lower energy inputs but faces uncertainty regarding the presence of hydrogen in economically viable accumulations and associated exploration costs, whereas stimulated geologic hydrogen could help address these uncertainties but would need to demonstrate net energy, emissions and economic advantages given the additional energy required for stimulation.

initiatives.description: ProtonH2 plans to repurpose oil infrastructure using controlled oxygen injection to oxidise residual hydrocarbons and produce hydrogen-rich syngas; its Apollo project targets 500 tpd H2 in Canada.

First subsurface bio-stimulated hydrogen production trial in a legacy oilfield yields a 40% H2 concentration in the resulting gas stream. In June 2025, Gold H2 completed a trial in California’s San Joaquin Basin using its proprietary biotechnology to stimulate microbial activity in a depleted oil well. The process involves flooding the reservoir with microbes, turning the well into a fermentation reactor where residual hydrocarbons are broken down into hydrogen and CO2. The resulting H2-rich gas is extracted, while the CO2 is either retained within the reservoir or sequestered through reinjection

tech_0510_natural-hydrogen-extraction.technology_status_summary_zh: 天然地質氫是指透過自然發生的地質與地球化學反應，在地下產生並累積的氫氣，天然氫萃取涉及直接從地下儲層回收此氫氣，無需合成轉化製程，科學界公認三種主要的地下生成機制：蛇紋岩化、輻射分解與深部地幔脫氣，蛇紋岩化目前被視為最主要的機制。
tech_0510_natural-hydrogen-extraction.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 地質氫正被探索作為以化石為基礎的製氫的替代方案，後者即使二氧化碳經減排，仍涉及燃料供應鏈的上游與中游排放，也是電解氫生產或其他可能涉及高生產成本路徑的替代方案，儘管多種地質氫路徑已經過測試。
tech_0510_natural-hydrogen-extraction.technology_status_summary_en:
Natural geologic hydrogen refers to hydrogen that is generated and accumulates in the subsurface through naturally occurring geological and geochemical reactions. Natural hydrogen extraction involves the direct recovery of this hydrogen from underground reservoirs, without the need for synthetic conversion processes. The scientific community recognises three main subsurface generation mechanisms: serpentinisation, radiolysis and deep mantle degassing. Serpentinisation is currently considered the most significant source, producing hydrogen through redox reactions in which water interacts with iron-rich rocks under elevated temperatures (200-400 °C), leading to the oxidation of ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) to ferric iron (Fe³⁺) and the reduction of water to molecular hydrogen. Radiolysis generates hydrogen when radiation from the decay of naturally occurring radioactive elements (uranium, thorium and potassium) splits water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen, particularly in quartz–feldspar-rich continental rocks. Deep mantle degassing may also contribute, with hydrogen migrating upwards along fault systems from deeper crustal or mantle sources.
Exploration strategies focus on geological settings where hydrogen generation is plausible, and where migration pathways, reservoirs, and sealing formations could enable accumulation. The exploration process is broadly similar to that for hydrocarbons. It starts with the identification of the source rock, followed by the migration pathways, and then the reservoirs and traps. Prospective resources are therefore expected to be found where iron-rich lithologies or naturally radioactive rocks coincide with deep-seated faults, low-permeability seals and limited biological or mineralogical consumption of hydrogen. Currently, the only commercial production is at the Bourakébougou field in Mali, producing around 5 tonnes per year. Large-scale commercial development before 2030 remains uncertain due to the limited understanding of generation, migration, and accumulation in commercially viable quantities, as well as the lack of established exploration tools. Production costs are therefore highly uncertain and will depend on reservoir concentration, ease of extraction and gas processing requirements, including the composition of co-produced gases. In this regard, some projects are exploring the potential for co-producing helium.
tech_0510_natural-hydrogen-extraction.market_dynamics_summary_en: Geologic hydrogen is being explored as an alternative to fossil-based hydrogen production, which would involve upstream and midstream emissions from fuel supply chains even when CO2 is abated, as well as to electrolytic hydrogen production or other routes that may entail high production costs. While several geologic hydrogen pathways have been tested in subsurface environments, they remain at an early stage of development, with significant uncertainties related to scalability, long-term production performance and economic viability. As a result, the role of geologic hydrogen remains uncertain and is likely to be limited in the near to medium term. Natural hydrogen extraction may involve lower energy inputs but faces uncertainty regarding the presence of hydrogen in economically viable accumulations and associated exploration costs, whereas stimulated geologic hydrogen could help address these uncertainties but would need to demonstrate net energy, emissions and economic advantages given the additional energy required for stimulation.

initiatives.description: In February 2024, US ARPA-E allocated USD 7.2 million to research projects focusing on subsurface engineering technologies relevant to the extraction of geologic hydrogen.

In 2025, Primary Hydrogen launched its exploration programme in Canada, beginning with satellite-based hyperspectral imaging, to be followed by field sampling and airborne or ground-based geophysical surveys to refine exploration targets.
The only commercial production geological hydrogen to date is at the Bourakebougou field in Mali (5 tpa, equivalent to 100 barrels of oil per year).
Helios Aragón is developing the Monzon-2 project, with appraisal drilling planned for H2 2025
Storengy and 45-8 ENERGY has secured exploration licenses for nearly 1 000 km2 in the south-west of France.
Mantle8 is developing AI tools to identify reservoirs in the Pyrenees
80 Mile reported significant surface hydrogen concentrations at legacy drill holes at its Hammaslahti project.
Desert Energy secured a 960 km2 exploration licence.
The US Geological Survey released its first hydrogen prospectivity map identifying areas with high potential for exploration.

HyTerra confirmed up to 96% hydrogen purity at its Nemaha Project in Kansas and began appraisal drilling, Top End Energy expanded its Kansas lease to 120 km2, while Koloma and PureWave Hydrogen continued exploration. Desert Mountain Energy plans exploration in Arizona and New Mexico, and Primary Hydrogen in Colorado.

In Canada, Max Power Mining announced 87% hydrogen purity in Saskatchewan, with 5 200 km2 permitted for exploration and 23 000 km2 under application. QMIC conducted a geochemical survey in Ontario-Quebec, began drilling and confirmed high hydrogen levels in Nova Scotia, where soil gas sampling started. In Quebec Squatex acquired 60 km2, and in Ontario DiagnaMed holds exploration permits and Record Resources acquired land. In British Columbia, a satellite survey confirmed hydrogen-rich zones on 22 000 km2, including Protium’s lands, and Primary Hydrogen plans exploration, and in Newfoundland First Atlantic Nickel also plans exploration.
The National Hydrocarbons Agency confirmed subsoil hydrogen reserves in the Cordillera Oriental and Sinú-San Jacinto basins.
Petrobras is investing around USD 4 million in exploratory research, initiated in Bahia in 2023 and planned for expansion.

In South Australia, Gold Hydrogen plans appraisal drilling at its Ramsay project by end-2025, D3 Energy has acquired exploration permits for nearly 6 000 km2, Whitebark Energy will launch geochemistry surveys in mid-2025, and Thor Energy has announced positive results at its Hy-Range project and added 6 300 km2 of exploration licences. In Tasmania, Devil Resources holds exploration licences for 6 000 km2.
The Philippines launched the world’s first natural hydrogen exploration auction in 2024, covering 2 300 km2, after confirming 800 tpa H2 at the Nagsasa seepage.
In Indonesia’s Sulawesi island, Pertamina Hulu Energi drilled three wells, with the Geological Agency confirming hydrogen at surface seepages.

tech_0511_mineralogical-reactions.technology_status_summary_zh: 刺激型地質氫是指透過人為介入刻意誘導或加速產氫地質與地球化學反應，在地下產生的氫氣，與透過自然發生地下過程產生的天然地質氫不同，此方法試圖利用工程技術重現或增強有利條件，而非仰賴發現天然累積的氫氣蘊藏，後者仍屬稀少。
tech_0511_mineralogical-reactions.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 地質氫正被探索作為以化石為基礎的製氫的替代方案，後者即使二氧化碳經減排，仍涉及燃料供應鏈的上游與中游排放，也是電解氫生產或其他可能涉及高生產成本路徑的替代方案，儘管多種地質氫路徑已經過測試。
tech_0511_mineralogical-reactions.technology_status_summary_en:
Stimulated geologic hydrogen refers to hydrogen generated in the subsurface by deliberately inducing or accelerating hydrogen-producing geological and geochemical reactions through anthropogenic interventions. Unlike natural geologic hydrogen, which is generated through naturally occurring subsurface processes, this approach seeks to recreate or enhance favourable conditions using engineered techniques. Rather than relying on the discovery of naturally accumulated hydrogen occurrences, which remain uncertain, stimulated approaches aim to improve predictability and reduce exploration risk by actively driving hydrogen generation. However, these approaches remain at low technology readiness levels, and significant uncertainties persist.
One group of approaches focuses on anthropogenic serpentinisation, which mirrors naturally occurring serpentinisation but seeks to increase hydrogen generation rates to potentially recoverable levels. Hydrogen is produced through redox reactions between water and iron-rich minerals, in which ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) is oxidised to ferric iron (Fe³⁺) and water is reduced to molecular hydrogen. Stimulation methods under investigation include the injection of water or reactive fluids into iron-rich rocks to promote serpentinisation reactions; thermal or hydraulic stimulation to increase permeability and expose fresh mineral surfaces, thereby increasing rock-water contact and enhancing reaction kinetics; and electrical reservoir stimulation, in which high-voltage electric currents are applied to generate localised heating and controlled fracturing to activate serpentinisation-related geochemical reactions.
Mineral carbonation-driven hydrogen production represents a distinct mineral-based pathway in ultrabasic rocks. In this configuration, hydrogen generation is coupled with mineral carbonation reactions, whereby injected fluids and CO2 react with minerals to form stable carbonate phases while hydrogen is produced as a co-product of associated redox reactions. This approach may offer dual benefits by combining hydrogen production with permanent CO2 sequestration.
tech_0511_mineralogical-reactions.market_dynamics_summary_en: Geologic hydrogen is being explored as an alternative to fossil-based hydrogen production, which would involve upstream and midstream emissions from fuel supply chains even when CO2 is abated, as well as to electrolytic hydrogen production or other routes that may entail high production costs. While several geologic hydrogen pathways have been tested in subsurface environments, they remain at an early stage of development, with significant uncertainties related to scalability, long-term production performance and economic viability. As a result, the role of geologic hydrogen remains uncertain and is likely to be limited in the near to medium term. Natural hydrogen extraction may involve lower energy inputs but faces uncertainty regarding the presence of hydrogen in economically viable accumulations and associated exploration costs, whereas stimulated geologic hydrogen could help address these uncertainties but would need to demonstrate net energy, emissions and economic advantages given the additional energy required for stimulation.

initiatives.description: GeoRedox launched its Advanced Weathering Enhancement technology for stimulated geologic hydrogen and partnered with Sage to develop a demonstration project.
US ARPA-E allocated USD 12.8 million to research projects focusing on production of geologic hydrogen through stimulated mineralogical processes.

tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.technology_status_summary_zh: 由鋁製氫是基於鋁水反應，鋁與水反應透過氧化還原過程生成氫氣，在此反應中，鋁被氧化，水被還原為氫氣，依溫度與壓力等運作條件形成氫氧化鋁或氧化鋁，此反應在常溫常壓下具熱力學可行性且高度放熱，釋放大量熱能，然而實務上鋁反應需要活化。
tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 由鋁水反應製氫的成熟度低於其他低排放製氫路徑，但因其探索將鋁作為可回收能源載體以產生氫氣與熱能（可儲存與運輸）而備受關注，其成本與排放表現高度取決於能否取得充足的低排放鋁。
tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.technology_status_summary_en:
Hydrogen production from aluminium is based on the aluminium–water reaction, in which aluminium reacts with water to generate hydrogen through a redox process. In this reaction, aluminium is oxidised while water is reduced to hydrogen, forming aluminium hydroxides or oxides depending on operating conditions such as temperature and pressure. The reaction is thermodynamically favourable at ambient conditions and highly exothermic, releasing significant amounts of heat. However, in practice aluminium is protected by a stable aluminium oxide (Al2O₃) layer that forms spontaneously on its surface and prevents direct contact between the metal and water, inhibiting the reaction.
To enable hydrogen generation, this passivating oxide layer must be disrupted or bypassed. Approaches investigated include chemical activation using alkaline or saline promoters (such as NaOH, KOH, NaCl or KCl), which can dissolve the oxide layer under ambient conditions but may introduce corrosion challenges. Other strategies rely on alloying aluminium with elements such as gallium or lithium, which inhibit oxide layer formation. Mechanical activation and the use of elevated temperatures or pressures have also been explored, although they increase energy requirements.
For aluminium-based hydrogen production to be economically viable, efficient recovery and recycling of aluminium is critical. After reaction with water, aluminium is converted primarily into aluminium hydroxide or alumina, which must be processed back into metallic aluminium through calcination followed by electrolytic reduction via the Hall–Héroult process. This regeneration step is energy-intensive and requires access to low-cost, low-emissions electricity. In addition, any alloying elements or chemical promoters used to enable the reaction must be recoverable at high rates to avoid excessive material losses and costs.
tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.market_dynamics_summary_en: Hydrogen production from aluminium-water reactions is less mature than other low-emissions hydrogen pathways but is of interest as it explores the use of aluminium as a recyclable energy carrier for hydrogen and heat generation, which can be stored and transported. Its cost and emissions performance depend critically on access to abundant low-emissions electricity for aluminium re-smelting.
tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.description:
Hydrogen production from aluminium is based on the aluminium–water reaction, in which aluminium reacts with water to generate hydrogen through a redox process. In this reaction, aluminium is oxidised while water is reduced to hydrogen, forming aluminium hydroxides or oxides depending on operating conditions such as temperature and pressure. The reaction is thermodynamically favourable at ambient conditions and highly exothermic, releasing significant amounts of heat. However, in practice aluminium is protected by a stable aluminium oxide (Al2O₃) layer that forms spontaneously on its surface and prevents direct contact between the metal and water, inhibiting the reaction.
To enable hydrogen generation, this passivating oxide layer must be disrupted or bypassed. Approaches investigated include chemical activation using alkaline or saline promoters (such as NaOH, KOH, NaCl or KCl), which can dissolve the oxide layer under ambient conditions but may introduce corrosion challenges. Other strategies rely on alloying aluminium with elements such as gallium or lithium, which inhibit oxide layer formation. Mechanical activation and the use of elevated temperatures or pressures have also been explored, although they increase energy requirements.
For aluminium-based hydrogen production to be economically viable, efficient recovery and recycling of aluminium is critical. After reaction with water, aluminium is converted primarily into aluminium hydroxide or alumina, which must be processed back into metallic aluminium through calcination followed by electrolytic reduction via the Hall–Héroult process. This regeneration step is energy-intensive and requires access to low-cost, low-emissions electricity. In addition, any alloying elements or chemical promoters used to enable the reaction must be recoverable at high rates to avoid excessive material losses and costs.
tech_0512_aluminum-water-reaction.NZErationale: Hydrogen production from aluminium-water reactions is less mature than other low-emissions hydrogen pathways but is of interest as it explores the use of aluminium as a recyclable energy carrier for hydrogen and heat generation, which can be stored and transported. Its cost and emissions performance depend critically on access to abundant low-emissions electricity for aluminium re-smelting.

initiatives.description: Boston-based startup Found Energy has successfully produced 20 kW of continuous, hydrogen-based thermal power in an experimental reactor  using 1kg  of aluminium scrap as a fuel source

The REVEAL project, co-funded by the EU Horizon Europe research and innovation programme and the Swiss State Secretariate for Education, Research and Innovation brings together 9 partners to develop a unique solution to energy storage, using the conversion of aluminium oxide into aluminium metal (Power-to-Al) in an environmentally friendly way to store renewable energy and produce a "renewable fuel" in the form of aluminium.

tech_0513_biological-hydrogen-production.technology_status_summary_zh: 生物製氫涵蓋一系列由微生物或生物酵素促成氫氣形成的路徑，這些路徑大致可分為以生質原料為基礎的製程（將生質原料衍生的有機物轉化為氫氣）與非生質原料製程（微生物作為光驅動水分解的生物觸媒），儘管兩者皆屬生物製氫的廣義範疇，僅以生質原料為基礎的路徑涉及有機物轉化。
tech_0513_biological-hydrogen-production.market_dynamics_summary_zh: 非熱化學生物製氫可從不適合熱化學轉化的濕潤與廢棄生質原料流中產生氫氣，由於這些製程在溫和的溫度與壓力下運作，可降低能源需求，並可與廢水處理系統整合，然而生物路徑的產率仍待提升。
tech_0513_biological-hydrogen-production.technology_status_summary_en:
Biological hydrogen production comprises a range of pathways in which microorganisms or biological enzymes enable hydrogen formation. These pathways can be broadly divided into biomass-based processes, where biomass-derived organic matter is converted into hydrogen, and non-biomass processes, where microorganisms act as biocatalysts for light-driven water splitting. While both fall under the broader category of biological hydrogen production, only biomass-based pathways involve the conversion of biomass feedstocks.
Non-thermochemical biomass use produces hydrogen through biological fermentation or electrochemical processes operating at low temperatures and pressures, avoiding the high energy inputs associated with thermochemical conversion. Unlike routes such as biomass gasification, these pathways rely on microbial or bio-electrochemical activity to extract hydrogen from wet, solid or liquid biomass streams, including agricultural residues, organic wastes and biomass-derived intermediates. Although generally less mature than thermochemical routes, they offer advantages such as compatibility with high-moisture feedstocks and potential integration with wastewater treatment.
Biological fermentative pathways convert biomass-derived organic substrates into hydrogen through enzymatic reactions. In dark fermentation, hydrogen is produced without requiring light by strict or facultative anaerobic bacteria such as Clostridium, Enterobacter and Bacillus species. These microorganisms metabolise carbohydrate-rich biomass (e.g. glucose), partially oxidising the substrate, producing hydrogen alongside CO2, organic acids (including acetic, butyric and lactic acids) and alcohols as by-products, through the action of hydrogenase enzymes. In photo-fermentation, photosynthetic bacteria - most notably purple non-sulfur (PNS) bacteria such as Rhodobacter, Rhodopseudomonas and Rhodospirillum - convert organic acids into hydrogen using light supplied by natural sunlight or artificial illumination. The process operates under anaerobic conditions and is driven primarily by nitrogenase enzymes under nitrogen-limited conditions, with hydrogenase playing a secondary role in redox balance. Dark and photo-fermentation can be combined in sequential dark/photo-fermentation systems, where organic acids from dark fermentation serve as substrates for photo-fermentation, improving overall biomass-to-hydrogen conversion efficiency.
Electrochemical pathways for hydrogen production from biomass use bio-electrochemical systems, most notably microbial electrolysis cells (MECs). In MECs, electroactive microorganisms grow on the anode and oxidise biodegradable organic compounds, such as organic acids, sugars or wastewater-derived substrates, releasing electrons and hydrogen-containing species. When a small external voltage is applied, electrons flow through an electrical circuit to the cathode, where hydrogen gas is produced. MECs operate under mild conditions and can valorise low-value or waste biomass streams. Common exoelectrogenic microorganisms include Escherichia coli, Geobacter, Shewanella, Pseudomonas, Rhodoferax and Citrobacter species. As MECs can directly use organic acids, they may be considered complementary to dark fermentation.
Non-biomass and hybrid biological hydrogen production pathways use microorganisms primarily as biocatalysts, with light as the main energy input. The main example is biophotolysis, which can be implemented as direct or indirect biophotolysis. In direct biophotolysis, photosynthetic microorganisms such as microalgae or cyanobacteria produce hydrogen directly from water under illumination, while oxygen is generated at the same time, strongly inhibiting hydrogenase activity and requiring oxygen concentrations to be maintained at very low levels. In indirect biophotolysis, hydrogen and oxygen production are separated: microorganisms first produce and store carbohydrates (e.g. glycogen or starch) under light conditions, and hydrogen is subsequently produced from these stored compounds through a photo-fermentation. This separation reduces enzyme deactivation by oxygen.
tech_0513_biological-hydrogen-production.market_dynamics_summary_en: Non-thermochemical biological hydrogen production enables hydrogen to be generated from wet and waste biomass streams that are not suitable for thermochemical conversion. As these processes operate at mild temperatures and pressures, they reduce energy requirements and allow integration with wastewater treatment systems. However, biological pathways are still in the early stages of development, and their deployment potential is currently limited by technological maturity and the availability of sustainable biomass feedstocks. The exception to this is biophotolysis, which does not require direct biomass input.
